proposals.title: Robust Prenatal Immune Signatures for PPROM via Anomaly-Aware Pathway Profiling
proposals.motivation: Preterm prelabor rupture of membranes (PPROM) is a major cause of preterm birth and neonatal morbidity. High-dimensional molecular profiling offers promise for biomarker discovery, but longitudinal studies are highly susceptible to batch effects and technical drift, which can obscure true biological signals, particularly in immune pathways. Robust methods are needed to distinguish technical artifacts from genuine biological variation to identify reliable clinical signatures.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that treating longitudinal multi-omics data as a sensor network and applying anomaly detection to pathway activity profiles will filter technical noise, enabling the identification of robust, leukocyte-mediated immune signatures predictive of PPROM.
proposals.method: We will model gene-set variation analysis (GSVA) scores for curated biological pathways as multi-stream sensor signals. Using pre-2026 transfer learning frameworks like Deep Autoencoding Gaussian Mixture Model (DAGMM) or Isolation Forest, we will train anomaly detection models on control samples and public datasets with known batch effects to identify technical deviations. The filtered, anomaly-corrected pathway activity matrices will then be analyzed using regularized regression (e.g., LASSO) and tree-based models (XGBoost) to identify predictive immune pathways for PPROM in a longitudinal prenatal cohort.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel computational pipeline for technical artifact removal in longitudinal omics, increasing the robustness of prenatal biomarker studies. It will yield a refined set of immune pathway signatures specifically associated with PPROM etiology, moving beyond confounded gene lists to actionable biological mechanisms.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the conceptual fusion of industrial sensor anomaly detection algorithms with functional pathway biology, a cross-disciplinary approach not previously applied to prenatal immunomics for de-noising and biomarker discovery.

proposals.title: Causal Triangulation of Preterm Birth: A Framework Integrating Genetic Instrumentation with Deep, Longitudinal Multi-Omic Profiling
proposals.motivation: Preterm birth (PTB) remains a leading cause of neonatal mortality and morbidity, yet its causal biological pathways are poorly defined. Traditional observational studies identify correlative biomarkers, while Mendelian randomization (MR) is often limited by pleiotropy and weak instruments for pregnancy-specific traits. A robust, multi-method causal inference framework is urgently needed to distinguish true drivers from epiphenomena.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating evidence from genetic causal inference (using instruments for maternal and fetal traits) with longitudinal, within-individual mediation analyses of serial multi-omic data will converge on a robust set of causal molecular pathways for PTB, overcoming the limitations of any single method.
proposals.method: We will leverage a deeply phenotyped prospective pregnancy cohort with serial blood samples. First, we will apply multivariable MR and colocalization using pre-2026 GWAS summary statistics to prioritize putatively causal exposures. Second, we will perform deep, longitudinal molecular profiling (transcriptomics, metabolomics, proteomics, immune cell phenotyping via mass cytometry) on the cohort. Third, we will use a mediation analysis framework, potentially with recurrent neural networks or structured equation modeling, to test if the molecular signatures implicated by MR mediate the effect of clinical risk factors on PTB outcome within the longitudinal data, creating a triangulation of evidence.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, open-source computational pipeline for causal triangulation in perinatal health. It will produce a shortlist of high-confidence causal pathways and biomarkers for PTB, moving the field beyond correlation. The framework establishes a blueprint for using prospective cohorts to validate and refine genetic causal inferences.
proposals.novelty: The key innovation is the formal integration of cross-sectional genetic instrumental variable methods with deep longitudinal mediation in the same cohort, creating a closed-loop system for causal discovery where each method's assumptions are tested and reinforced by the other.

proposals.title: Identifying and Mechanistically Validating High-Leverage Control Nodes in the Multi-Omic Network of Pregnancy to Prevent Preterm Birth
proposals.motivation: Preterm birth (PTB) remains a leading cause of neonatal morbidity and mortality. Current predictive models, including those based on omics data, are largely correlative and do not identify causal, high-impact points for intervention. A paradigm shift from risk prediction to identifying system-level control points is needed to develop targeted, mechanism-based prevention strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying network control theory to a high-dimensional multi-omic model of pregnancy will identify a sparse set of biologically plausible 'control nodes' (e.g., specific immune cell states or microbial metabolites), and that targeted experimental stabilization of these nodes will prevent inflammation-driven PTB in preclinical models.
proposals.method: We will construct a dynamic, patient-specific multi-omic network (transcriptomic, proteomic, metabolomic, microbiome) from serial maternal samples using graph neural networks (GNNs) to model interactions. We will then apply network control theory algorithms (e.g., minimum driver node set identification) to this topology to solve the inverse problem and pinpoint high-leverage control nodes. These predicted nodes will be directly perturbed (e.g., via monoclonal antibodies, metabolite supplementation, or genetic knockdown) in a murine model of lipopolysaccharide-induced PTB to test their causal role in preventing the pathological cascade.
proposals.contribution: This work will move the field from correlative PTB risk scores to a causal, control-theoretic framework for intervention. It will deliver a validated, shortlist of high-impact biological targets for stabilization. The methodology establishes a generalizable pipeline for translating predictive omic networks into testable mechanistic hypotheses for complex diseases.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct application of engineering-derived network control theory to a clinical obstetrics problem, repurposing the predictive model not for prognosis but to solve for optimal intervention points, followed by immediate causal validation in a relevant biological system.

proposals.title: A Multi-Omic and Clinical Machine Learning Framework for the Early Prediction of Post-Term Birth and Prolonged Pregnancy
proposals.motivation: Post-term pregnancy, defined as gestation exceeding 42 weeks, is associated with increased risks of perinatal mortality, meconium aspiration, and labor complications. Current clinical methods for predicting prolonged pregnancy are imprecise, leading to inefficient monitoring and intervention strategies. This represents a critical, understudied counterpart to the more commonly researched problem of preterm birth.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a multi-modal machine learning model integrating maternal immune cell profiling, metabolomic/lipidomic data, and clinical characteristics can identify early-pregnancy signatures predictive of post-term birth with high accuracy.
proposals.method: We will apply a deep learning framework analogous to our Alzheimer's disease resilience work to maternal peripheral blood mononuclear cell (PBMC) CyTOF data and serum metabolomic/lipidomic profiles from cohorts like the IMiC Consortium. Clinical variables (e.g., maternal age, BMI, parity) will be integrated. A specific neural network architecture, such as a multi-input deep classifier or a graph neural network modeling cell-cell interactions, will be trained to predict delivery beyond 41+0 weeks. Model performance will be validated against held-out test sets.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first predictive model for post-term birth using deep learning on multi-omic data, identifying novel biological pathways associated with prolonged gestation. It will provide a clinical tool for risk stratification, enabling personalized antenatal care plans. The framework will also establish a 'dual' analytical paradigm for pregnancy duration, complementing models for preterm birth.
proposals.novelty: While machine learning has been applied to preterm birth, its application to predict prolonged pregnancy using deep learning on immune cell states and metabolomics is novel. The approach directly translates our group's established methods in neurodegeneration and placenta accreta to a new, opposite clinical endpoint in perinatal health.

proposals.title: Deconvolving the Maternal Immune Clock: A Single-Cell Atlas for Predicting Pregnancy Outcomes
proposals.motivation: Pregnancy complications like preeclampsia and preterm birth are major drivers of maternal and neonatal morbidity, yet their prediction remains imprecise. While bulk immune signatures from maternal blood show promise as predictive 'clocks', they are composite signals that obscure the specific cellular drivers and states. Resolving this signal into its fundamental immune components is critical for moving from correlation to mechanistic understanding and actionable clinical biomarkers.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the predictive power of bulk maternal immune signatures for adverse pregnancy outcomes is driven by specific, rare immune cell subsets and their coordinated activation states, which can be identified and quantified through high-resolution single-cell profiling.
proposals.method: We will perform single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) with cellular indexing of transcriptomes and epitopes (CITE-seq) on serial maternal blood samples from a large, deeply phenotyped pregnancy cohort. Using a custom analysis pipeline, we will apply graph-based clustering (e.g., Leiden algorithm) and reference mapping (e.g., with Azimuth) to define cell subtypes. We will then train interpretable machine learning models (e.g., logistic regression with LASSO, gradient-boosted trees) on cell state proportions and gene module scores to predict outcomes, followed by trajectory inference (e.g., Monocle3, PAGA) to model dynamic changes.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first high-resolution, causal atlas of maternal immune dynamics linked to pregnancy health, identifying precise cellular targets for intervention. It will deliver a validated, deconvolved predictive model that surpasses the resolution of bulk assays, providing a framework for translating composite immune signatures into specific diagnostic and therapeutic strategies.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying a spectroscopic deconvolution analogy to a clinical prediction problem, using scRNA-seq not just for discovery but explicitly to resolve and attribute predictive signal to specific cell states and trajectories, moving beyond bulk correlations to a causal cellular map.

proposals.title: Causal Inference of Immune Dysregulation in Placental Accreta Spectrum: A Multi-Omic, Deep Learning Approach
proposals.motivation: Placental Accreta Spectrum (PAS) is a life-threatening obstetrical condition with rising incidence. While immune dysregulation is a hallmark, it remains unknown whether these changes are primary drivers of abnormal placentation or secondary consequences of placental stress. Disentangling this causality is critical for developing targeted interventions and predictive biomarkers that address root causes, not just downstream effects.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a specific, early-gestation immune cell state, identifiable through high-dimensional profiling, is a causal driver of PAS pathology, preceding and contributing to the development of placental stress and invasive trophoblast phenotype.
proposals.method: We will leverage existing and prospectively collected maternal blood samples from first and early second trimester. Using mass cytometry (CyTOF) for deep immune phenotyping and paired plasma metabolomic/lipidomic profiling, we will construct a multi-omic dataset. A causal deep learning framework, building on architectures like Causal Transformer Networks or Structural Causal Models integrated with variational autoencoders, will be trained to infer temporal and causal relationships between immune features, metabolomic shifts, and eventual PAS diagnosis, controlling for clinical confounders.
proposals.contribution: This work will move the field from association to causal inference in PAS immunology, identifying potential upstream immune therapeutic targets. It will deliver a causal, multi-omic model that improves early risk stratification beyond current clinical models, directly addressing a critical gap in maternal-fetal medicine.
proposals.novelty: While prior work (including our own) has identified PAS-associated immune signatures, this study is the first to explicitly model the directionality of effect using a pre-pathology temporal design and causal deep learning, moving beyond correlative analyses to test a specific mechanistic hypothesis.

proposals.title: A Multi-Omic Machine Learning Framework to Decipher the Role of STAT5ab Signaling in CD4+ T Cell Dysfunction in Preeclampsia
proposals.motivation: Preeclampsia (PE) is a leading cause of maternal and fetal morbidity, yet its pathogenesis remains incompletely understood. Dysregulated maternal immune tolerance, particularly in CD4+ T cell subsets, is implicated, but specific signaling pathways driving this dysfunction are unclear. Pre-2026 work has identified STAT5ab signaling as a key regulator of T cell function and differentiation, and preliminary evidence suggests its dysregulation may be associated with adverse pregnancy outcomes, creating a critical knowledge gap.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that abnormal STAT5ab signaling in CD4+ T cells drives a pathogenic shift in their functional and metabolic profiles, directly contributing to the systemic inflammation and endothelial dysfunction characteristic of preeclampsia.
proposals.method: We will apply a multi-omic, deep learning-based approach to CD4+ T cells isolated from PE patients and matched controls. Using mass cytometry (CyTOF) and single-cell RNA sequencing, we will profile signaling (pSTAT5), metabolic state, and transcriptomes. A graph neural network (GNN) architecture will be trained to integrate these data layers and identify specific cell subpopulations and signaling networks associated with abnormal STAT5. We will validate functional consequences using in vitro assays modulating STAT5 activity in primary T cells and correlating findings with clinical severity from the IMiC consortium-style harmonized patient metadata.
proposals.contribution: This work will define a mechanistic link between a specific signaling aberration in immune cells and PE pathogenesis, moving beyond association to causality. It will deliver a validated, interpretable machine learning model that identifies high-risk immune signatures, providing a foundation for novel diagnostic and therapeutic strategies targeting immune dysfunction in pregnancy.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies associate immune dysfunction with PE, this proposal uniquely combines deep learning-based cell state profiling with direct functional interrogation of the STAT5ab pathway in a specific, clinically relevant cell type, aiming to uncover a precise, targetable mechanism.

proposals.title: Causal Immune Subtyping of Preeclampsia via Endophenotype-Stratified Mendelian Randomization
proposals.motivation: Preeclampsia is a leading cause of maternal and fetal morbidity and mortality, yet it is a clinically heterogeneous syndrome with no effective primary prevention or cure. Current classifications (e.g., early vs. late onset) are descriptive and do not reflect distinct underlying pathophysiologies, hindering the development of targeted therapies. The role of immune dysregulation is strongly implicated but remains poorly understood in a causal, subtype-specific context.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the clinical heterogeneity of preeclampsia arises from distinct causal immune mechanisms, which can be delineated by applying Mendelian randomization within patient strata defined by specific endophenotypes (e.g., angiogenic imbalance, placental pathology).
proposals.method: We will leverage large-scale genetic association data (e.g., from UK Biobank, FinnGen, and immune cell QTL studies) to construct genetic instruments for specific immune traits, including cytokine levels and deep immunophenotyping profiles (e.g., from mass cytometry). Using two-sample Mendelian randomization (primarily inverse-variance weighted and MR-Egger methods), we will test the causal effect of each immune trait on preeclampsia risk, first in an unstratified analysis and then within strata defined by endophenotypes derived from clinical and molecular data (e.g., sFlt-1/PlGF ratio, histopathological findings). Machine learning (unsupervised clustering on causal estimate profiles) will be used to define final causal subtypes.
proposals.contribution: This work will move beyond correlation to establish a causal taxonomy of preeclampsia based on divergent immune pathways. It will directly link genetic predisposition for specific immune dysfunctions to distinct disease manifestations. The resulting subtypes will provide a biologically grounded framework for stratifying patients in future clinical trials and for developing mechanism-specific interventions.
proposals.novelty: While Mendelian randomization has been applied to preeclampsia risk factors, this is the first proposal to use it as a tool for causal subtyping by integrating immune genetic instruments with endophenotypic stratification, moving from a 'one-disease' model to a causal taxonomy.

proposals.title: Design and Cost-Effectiveness Analysis of a Population-Level Prenatal Screening Program for Placenta Accreta Spectrum Using Maternal Immune Cell Signatures
proposals.motivation: Placenta Accreta Spectrum (PAS) is a life-threatening obstetrical complication associated with severe maternal morbidity and mortality. Current prenatal detection relies on ultrasound, which has variable sensitivity and is operator-dependent, leading to missed diagnoses and unprepared deliveries. A scalable, objective screening tool integrated into routine prenatal care could enable early identification and improve outcomes by ensuring planned, multidisciplinary deliveries.
proposals.hypothesis: That a machine learning model trained on maternal peripheral blood immune cell profiles and clinical data can be deployed as a cost-effective, first-tier population screening tool for PAS, identifying high-risk pregnancies earlier than standard imaging.
proposals.method: We will leverage a multi-center cohort with existing CyTOF or spectral flow cytometry data on maternal immune cells from PAS cases and controls. We will train an ensemble model (e.g., XGBoost or a deep neural network) on these high-dimensional immune signatures integrated with basic clinical variables (e.g., prior C-section, placenta previa). Using this validated model, we will design a screening protocol for implementation at the first prenatal visit and conduct a microsimulation cost-effectiveness analysis from a healthcare payer perspective, comparing the proposed immune signature screen against standard care.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first blueprint for a population-level, immune-based prenatal screening program, moving beyond diagnostic biomarker discovery to practical implementation. It will deliver a validated predictive model and a detailed analysis of its projected clinical impact, resource requirements, and cost-effectiveness, offering a tangible pathway for health system adoption.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has identified immune signatures associated with PAS, this proposal uniquely focuses on scaling these findings into a feasible screening program, integrating advanced machine learning with health economic modeling to bridge the gap between biomarker research and public health implementation.

proposals.title: A Paired Deep Learning Framework Reveals Maternal-Fetal Immune Cross-Talk Signatures Predictive of Neonatal Health Outcomes
proposals.motivation: The maternal and fetal immune systems engage in a complex, coordinated dialogue critical for a healthy pregnancy, yet this cross-compartmental communication remains poorly characterized at a systems level. Pre-2026, most perinatal immunology studies analyze maternal blood or cord blood in isolation, missing the paired regulatory dynamics. A comprehensive, paired analysis is needed to identify immune signatures of healthy adaptation versus dysregulation linked to adverse neonatal outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific, coordinated cell state changes across paired maternal and cord blood immune compartments form a measurable 'cross-talk signature' that is more predictive of neonatal health status (e.g., infection risk, gestational age at birth) than features from either compartment alone.
proposals.method: We will perform high-dimensional immunophenotyping (mass cytometry) on paired maternal peripheral blood and cord blood samples from a well-characterized clinical cohort. A dual-branch neural network architecture (e.g., a Siamese or coupled autoencoder network) will be trained to learn a joint latent representation of the paired samples. This model will integrate cell type frequencies, signaling states, and clinical metadata to identify cross-compartmental interaction patterns. Supervised learning models (e.g., gradient boosting) will then use these learned representations to predict specific neonatal health outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel computational framework for analyzing paired biological compartments, moving beyond single-compartment profiling. It will deliver the first high-resolution map of maternal-fetal immune cross-talk in a clinical cohort and identify specific, translatable immune signatures associated with neonatal resilience or vulnerability.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the paired, dual-compartment analysis using a bespoke deep learning architecture designed to model interaction, rather than simply comparing two independent datasets. This approach directly tests the system-level hypothesis of immune coordination, which has been inferred but not computationally modeled in prior work.

proposals.title: Longitudinal Single-Cell Immune Trajectory Inference for Dynamic Prediction of Preterm Birth Prevention Response
proposals.motivation: Preterm birth (PTB) remains a leading cause of neonatal mortality and morbidity. While preventive interventions like progesterone exist, response is heterogeneous, and current clinical stratification is static and imprecise. A dynamic, mechanistic understanding of how immune cell states evolve in response to therapy is needed to predict efficacy and identify novel targets for non-responders.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that successful PTB prevention is driven by specific, modifiable trajectories of maternal immune cell subtypes (e.g., monocyte de-priming, Treg stabilization), and that pre-treatment single-cell states can dynamically predict therapeutic response.
proposals.method: We will apply single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) to longitudinal maternal blood samples from a completed PTB prevention trial (e.g., progesterone). Using pre-2026 tools (Seurat, Monocle3, CellRank), we will construct unified cell state manifolds and infer probabilistic trajectories. A neural ODE-based model (inspired by pre-2026 work like Neural Ordinary Differential Equations) will be trained to learn differential trajectory dynamics between responders and non-responders, integrating baseline clinical covariates for dynamic outcome prediction.
proposals.contribution: This work will move the field from static biomarker signatures to a causal, dynamic model of preventive therapy. It will deliver a clinically actionable tool for early, dynamic response prediction and will nominate specific cell-type-resolved molecular pathways (e.g., ligand-receptor pairs) as novel targets for adjunctive therapy in predicted non-responders.
proposals.novelty: This integrates longitudinal scRNA-seq with clinical trial response data to model intervention-altered immune trajectories, a significant advance over cross-sectional case-control studies. The application of neural ODEs to learn differential cellular dynamics from patient-level time series data for clinical prediction is novel in reproductive immunology.

proposals.title: A Multi-Omic, Deep Learning Framework to Identify Lockdown-Associated Immune and Environmental Drivers of Reduced Preterm Birth
proposals.motivation: Observational studies from the COVID-19 pandemic lockdowns reported small but significant reductions in preterm birth rates. This presents a unique natural experiment to identify modifiable environmental and social determinants of pregnancy health. Understanding the specific causal mechanisms—such as reduced air pollution, altered maternal stress, or decreased infection exposure—is critical for developing targeted public health interventions to prevent preterm birth, a leading cause of neonatal mortality and morbidity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the reduction in preterm birth during lockdowns was driven by specific, measurable changes in the maternal immune milieu and exposure profile, and that these changes can be identified through integrated multi-omic and clinical data analysis.
proposals.method: We will leverage existing maternal cohorts with biospecimens (plasma, peripheral blood mononuclear cells) collected pre-, during, and post-lockdown. Using mass cytometry (CyTOF) and plasma metabolomic/lipidomic profiling, we will generate high-dimensional immune cell and molecular data. A multi-modal deep learning model, based on a variational autoencoder (VAE) architecture, will be trained to learn a joint embedding of these data with clinical and environmental variables (e.g., pollution data, mobility metrics). Supervised models, including attention-based neural networks, will then identify specific cell states and metabolic pathways most predictive of term delivery in the lockdown period.
proposals.contribution: This work will move beyond association to pinpoint the specific immune and environmental factors most strongly linked to the observed protective effect. It will deliver a validated, interpretable machine learning model that identifies high-risk pregnancies based on modifiable exposures and immune signatures. The findings will provide actionable targets for clinical and public health strategies aimed at reducing preterm birth.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies noted the epidemiological trend, this proposal uniquely applies a deep learning-based multi-omic integration framework, central to Dr. Aghaeepour's pre-2026 work, to dissect its mechanistic drivers. It treats the lockdown not just as an event, but as a perturbation experiment to decode the complex interplay between environment, immunity, and pregnancy outcome.

proposals.title: A Multi-Omic and Machine Learning Framework for Patient Stratification in a Platform Trial for Stillbirth Prevention in LMICs
proposals.motivation: Stillbirths remain a critical global health disparity, with over three-quarters occurring in low-resource settings. Observational studies and isolated trials have failed to identify scalable, context-specific intervention bundles. There is an urgent need for high-quality, causal evidence on effective, low-cost strategies that can be integrated into existing LMIC health systems.
proposals.hypothesis: That a pragmatic platform RCT, augmented by deep learning-based patient stratification using accessible clinical and molecular data, can efficiently identify which combinations of low-cost interventions (e.g., aspirin, infection protocols, community screening) are most effective for specific subpopulations of pregnant individuals, thereby reducing stillbirth rates.
proposals.method: We will design an adaptive, multi-arm platform RCT in LMICs. To move beyond a 'one-size-fits-all' analysis, we will integrate a machine learning component for causal inference and patient stratification. Using clinical data and accessible biospecimens (e.g., blood, potentially milk lipidomics as a proxy for maternal metabolic state), we will apply unsupervised deep learning (e.g., variational autoencoders) to identify latent patient endotypes. We will then use Bayesian adaptive randomization and causal forest algorithms to estimate heterogeneous treatment effects (HTEs) for different intervention bundles across these learned patient strata, dynamically informing trial adaptation.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate the first high-certainty evidence for scalable intervention packages to prevent stillbirths in LMICs. Methodologically, it will pioneer the integration of causal machine learning for patient stratification within a large-scale platform trial, creating a hybrid trial-design framework that efficiently matches interventions to patient subtypes in low-resource contexts.
proposals.novelty: The fusion of a pragmatic platform trial—a gold-standard for causal evaluation of multiple interventions—with a deep learning-based stratification strategy tailored for low-resource settings is novel. It moves beyond testing single interventions to dynamically identifying synergistic bundles for specific immune-metabolic patient endotypes derived from limited, feasible data streams.

proposals.title: A Multi-omic, Neighborhood-Resolved Biosensor for Placental Health: Identifying a Minimal Blood-Based Signature of Environmental and Social Stressor Synergy
proposals.motivation: Adverse birth outcomes are driven by a complex interplay of environmental and social stressors, which vary dramatically at the neighborhood level. Current risk assessments lack granular, biologically grounded tools to measure this synergistic exposure burden, hindering the identification of high-risk micro-environments and the evaluation of place-based public health interventions. A cost-effective, point-of-care diagnostic panel reflecting this multi-factorial burden is critically needed to advance environmental health equity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a minimal set of proteins and transcripts in maternal or cord blood, identifiable via multi-omic profiling, will form a robust biosignature that captures the synergistic impact of hyper-local environmental and social exposures on placental health and birth outcomes.
proposals.method: We will leverage existing maternal cohorts with paired biospecimens (plasma, RNA), detailed birth outcome data, and hyper-local environmental/social data (e.g., satellite-derived PM2.5, noise maps, census tract metrics). Using a multi-stage machine learning pipeline, we will first apply graph convolutional networks to model neighborhood-level exposure synergy. We will then use regularized regression (LASSO, elastic net) and feature selection on bulk RNA-seq and Olink proteomic data to identify a minimal predictive biomarker panel. This panel will be validated for clinical viability using simpler assays (e.g., targeted MS, qPCR) on an independent cohort.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated, minimal multi-omic panel that serves as a practical biosensor for neighborhood-level environmental health risk. It will provide a novel framework for fusing spatial epidemiology with biomarker discovery, creating a tool for community health screening and for quantitatively assessing the efficacy of targeted interventions aimed at reducing birth outcome disparities.
proposals.novelty: This approach moves beyond associating single exposures with broad outcomes by using advanced spatial ML to model exposure synergy at a fine-grained level, directly driving the discovery of a clinically actionable, multi-omic biomarker panel from blood, a minimally invasive source.

proposals.title: A Dual-System Machine Learning Framework for Predicting Postpartum Mental Health Trajectories from Synchronized Biophysical and Clinical Data
proposals.motivation: Maternal mental health disorders are a leading cause of maternal morbidity, yet they remain difficult to predict and are often diagnosed postnatally. Current predictive models in obstetrics focus predominantly on physical birth outcomes, treating mental health as a separate, often secondary, concern. This fragmented approach fails to capture the interdependent, longitudinal dynamics between a mother's physical health system and her parallel, vulnerable mental health system during the perinatal period.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a dual-system computational model, trained on synchronized longitudinal data from pregnancy through the postpartum period, can identify distinct, predictive signatures of adverse maternal mental health trajectories by modeling the 'load' of pregnancy and birth complications on an interdependent mental health system.
proposals.method: We will leverage existing and prospectively collected longitudinal cohorts with synchronized data streams: 1) the 'primary system' (maternal physical health/birth outcomes: clinical characteristics, immune cell profiles via mass cytometry, metabolomic/lipidomic data, placental pathology), and 2) the 'parallel system' (maternal mental health: serial EPDS scores, neuroendocrine profiles like cortisol, inflammatory cytokines). We will develop a multi-modal deep learning architecture, likely a dual-path recurrent neural network (RNN) or transformer with cross-attention mechanisms, to model temporal interactions between these systems. The model will be trained to predict postpartum depression/anxiety trajectories, using techniques from our group's prior work on resilience signatures in Alzheimer's disease.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, holistic computational framework for perinatal health that moves beyond single-outcome prediction. It will deliver a set of interpretable, multi-modal biomarkers that signal risk for mental health complications before they are clinically apparent. The methodology establishes a new paradigm for 'structural health monitoring' in human biology, applicable to other conditions where physical and psychological systems interact.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the formal biophysical analogy—treating mental health as a parallel, degrading system under load—and its computational instantiation via a synchronized dual-system model. This integrates disparate data types (immune, metabolic, clinical, psychological) into a single predictive framework for mental health, a synthesis not attempted in pre-2026 perinatal ML research.

proposals.title: A Multi-Omic and Clinical Machine Learning Framework for Predicting Postpartum Depression from Prenatal Immune and Metabolic Profiles
proposals.motivation: Maternal mental health, particularly postpartum depression (PPD), is a major public health crisis with profound impacts on both mother and child, yet its biological underpinnings remain poorly understood. Pre-2026 work by Aghaeepour and others has successfully used multi-omic profiling to predict adverse birth outcomes, but a parallel, integrated analysis of maternal mental health trajectories during the same perinatal period is lacking. This represents a critical missed opportunity to identify shared or divergent biological pathways influencing physical and mental health.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific prenatal immune cell state profiles and metabolic signatures, derived from maternal blood, are associated with and can predict the subsequent development of postpartum depression, and that these biological features will be partially distinct from those associated with adverse birth outcomes.
proposals.method: We will leverage existing and prospectively collected longitudinal cohorts with paired clinical metadata, flow/mass cytometry immunophenotyping, and metabolomic/lipidomic profiling from maternal blood samples taken during each trimester. Using a multi-task deep learning architecture (e.g., a shared encoder with task-specific heads), we will jointly model the risk for PPD (primary task) and adverse birth outcomes (secondary task). Model inputs will be integrated multi-omic features, and we will employ attention mechanisms or Shapley Additive Explanations (SHAP) to identify the most predictive immune cell populations and metabolic pathways.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first predictive, biologically interpretable model of PPD risk based on prenatal multi-omic data, moving beyond purely clinical risk factors. It will identify specific immune and metabolic mechanisms potentially involved in PPD pathogenesis, offering novel targets for therapeutic intervention and enabling earlier, more personalized risk stratification.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct application of Aghaeepour's established multi-omic predictive pipeline—previously focused on placental disorders and birth outcomes—to the parallel challenge of maternal mental health, using a dual-task learning framework to explicitly model shared and unique biological drivers.

proposals.title: A Natural Experiment in Perinatal Immunology: Linking Elective Early-Term Delivery to Reduced Placental Cellular Diversity and Adverse Outcomes via Deep Histopathological Phenotyping
proposals.motivation: The COVID-19 pandemic created a unique, population-scale shift in obstetric care, with a documented increase in elective early-term deliveries to mitigate hospital exposure risk. This provides an unprecedented natural experiment to test a fundamental biological principle in human pregnancy: whether the physiological process of spontaneous labor is necessary for generating optimal cellular diversity in the placenta, a key immune-endocrine organ. Grounded in pre-2026 ecological theories of tissue fitness, this study directly investigates how a clinical management decision impacts tissue-level cellular ecology and subsequent health outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: Placentas from pregnancies electively delivered early, without the signaling cascade of spontaneous labor, will exhibit lower phenotypic diversity and a more clonal cellular architecture across trophoblast and stromal compartments, and this reduction in diversity will be directly associated with suboptimal neonatal and maternal postpartum outcomes.
proposals.method: We will analyze archived, digitized whole-slide images (WSIs) of placental histopathology from three cohorts: pre-pandemic (2019), pandemic lockdown (2020), and post-lockdown (2021-22). Using a deep learning framework based on pre-trained convolutional neural networks (e.g., ResNet50) for feature extraction, we will perform instance segmentation and phenotypic profiling of nuclei to generate high-dimensional embeddings for each cell. We will then quantify tissue-level diversity using ecological metrics (Shannon index, Pielou's evenness) and clonality using graph-based community detection algorithms on cell phenotype neighborhoods. These computed metrics will be integrated with clinical outcomes via gradient-boosted tree models (XGBoost) to identify specific diversity signatures predictive of adverse events.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct evidence linking a population-level healthcare intervention to quantifiable changes in human tissue ecology and clinical resilience. It will establish a novel analytical framework for applying ecological and diversity metrics to digital pathology in perinatal health. The findings could fundamentally inform guidelines on the timing of elective delivery by highlighting the biological cost of circumventing spontaneous labor.
proposals.novelty: While Dr. Aghaeepour's prior work uses ML to predict placental pathology, this study uniquely leverages a natural experiment to test a causal ecological hypothesis, moving from association to mechanism. The fusion of ecological diversity indices with deep learning-based cellular phenotyping in placental pathology is a novel methodological advance for the field.

proposals.title: A deep learning framework identifies a stabilized epithelial-mesenchymal hybrid state as a novel biomarker for placental maladaptation and stillbirth risk in low-resource settings
proposals.motivation: Stillbirth rates remain disproportionately high in low and middle-income countries (LMICs), with many cases classified as 'unexplained' due to a lack of specific molecular pathology. Pre-2026 work highlights placental maladaptation to environmental stressors as a key factor, but robust, mechanistically defined biomarkers for high-risk pregnancies in these settings are critically lacking. This gap hinders the development of targeted screening and prevention strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: Chronic exposure to infections, malnutrition, and inflammation in LMICs induces a maladaptive, stabilized epithelial-mesenchymal hybrid state, characterized by co-expression of E-cadherin and vimentin in placental cells, which serves as a direct biomarker of placental dysfunction and elevated stillbirth risk, independent of traditional etiologies.
proposals.method: We will apply a multi-modal deep learning pipeline to high-plex imaging mass cytometry (IMC) data from a retrospective cohort of placental tissues from stillbirth and live-birth controls across diverse LMICs. A convolutional neural network (CNN) will perform automated segmentation of trophoblasts and decidual cells. For each cell, expression levels of E-cadherin, vimentin, and other lineage/functional markers will be extracted. An unsupervised graph neural network (GNN) will model cell-cell interactions and spatial context to identify stabilized hybrid cell communities. Their prevalence will be correlated with clinical outcomes and histopathological diagnoses using supervised learning models (e.g., XGBoost).
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, mechanistically informed molecular subtype of stillbirth rooted in dysregulated cellular plasticity. It will deliver an interpretable, AI-driven biomarker for placental health that moves beyond syndromic classification. The findings will identify targetable pathways related to cellular stress adaptation, offering a new lens for risk stratification and prevention in global maternal health.
proposals.novelty: While EMT is studied in cancer, its role as a maladaptive, persistent state in placental pathology driven by LMIC-specific environmental stressors is unexplored. The approach uniquely fuses deep learning-based spatial single-cell analysis with global health epidemiology to discover context-specific disease subtypes.

proposals.title: Assessing the Sufficiency of Peripheral Blood Immunomics for Placenta Accreta Spectrum Disorder Prediction: A Multi-Tissue Machine Learning Analysis
proposals.motivation: Placenta Accreta Spectrum (PAS) is a life-threatening obstetrical complication with rising incidence. Current research, including Dr. Aghaeepour's prior work, leverages peripheral blood metabolomics and clinical data for PAS prediction. However, the disease is defined by abnormal trophoblast invasion into the uterine myometrium, suggesting that systemic blood measurements may miss critical local biological signals. In the pre-2026 era of precision medicine, it is essential to determine whether minimally invasive biomarkers are truly sufficient or if they provide an incomplete picture of localized pathology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that predictive models for PAS built solely on peripheral blood omics and clinical data will be significantly outperformed by models that also incorporate omics data (e.g., transcriptomics, proteomics) derived directly from the placenta and/or uterine implantation site tissue.
proposals.method: We will analyze a matched cohort of PAS and control patients with multi-tissue samples: peripheral blood, placenta, and uterine tissue. For each sample type, we will generate high-dimensional omics profiles (transcriptomics via RNA-seq, targeted proteomics). We will train and compare three deep learning architectures: 1) a model using only blood-based features, 2) a model using only tissue-based features, and 3) an integrated multi-modal model using all data. Architectures will include attention-based neural networks to identify feature importance and a late-fusion framework for the integrated model. Performance will be rigorously evaluated via nested cross-validation on held-out test sets.
proposals.contribution: This work will directly test the sufficiency of peripheral blood for modeling a localized obstetrical disease, providing a methodological framework for similar questions in other conditions. It will identify whether key predictive signals are systemic or tissue-resident, guiding future biomarker discovery and clinical trial design. The results will establish best practices for data collection in perinatal precision health studies.
proposals.novelty: While multi-omics integration is common, this proposal systematically challenges a foundational assumption in perinatal immunomics—that peripheral blood is a adequate proxy for local tissue states in placenta-mediated disorders. The direct, matched multi-tissue comparison for PAS prediction is novel and moves beyond correlation to test causal inference of biomarker origin.

proposals.title: Extreme Value Theory for Multiomic Signatures: A Novel Framework for Predicting Preterm Birth as a Developmental Extreme Event
proposals.motivation: Preterm birth (PTB) is a leading cause of neonatal mortality and morbidity, yet its prediction remains a major clinical challenge. Standard case-control analyses often fail to capture the specific dynamics of this high-risk, low-probability outcome, analogous to how standard climate models struggle with rare, high-impact weather events. A paradigm shift is needed to specifically model the 'tail' of the gestational age distribution.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying extreme value theory (EVT) to high-dimensional multiomic data (e.g., immunomics, metabolomics) will identify robust precursor signatures of PTB that are distinct from those found by standard comparative methods, enabling more accurate risk stratification for this extreme outcome.
proposals.method: We will analyze a large, PTB-enriched multiomic cohort (e.g., maternal blood, placenta) from existing studies. Instead of standard classification, we will model gestational age at delivery using EVT's Generalized Pareto Distribution to characterize the statistical 'tail' of early parturition. We will integrate this with a deep learning framework (e.g., a variational autoencoder) to learn latent multiomic representations. The model will be trained to jointly optimize the EVT likelihood for extreme events and a predictive loss, actively identifying patterns that signal entry into the extreme risk regime.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, risk-tailored computational framework for PTB prediction, moving beyond associative biomarkers to a model of the event's probability distribution. It will produce a set of high-specificity multiomic 'extreme event' signatures and provide a methodological blueprint for applying climate science-inspired analytics to other rare, high-impact health outcomes.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct translation of extreme value theory from climate science to developmental biology and the explicit modeling of PTB as a statistical extreme, rather than a binary classification problem, within a multiomic deep learning context.

proposals.title: Robust Identification of High-Risk Preterm Birth Phenotypes via Anomaly-Aware Multiomics Integration
proposals.motivation: Preterm birth (PTB) is a leading cause of neonatal mortality and morbidity, yet its biological mechanisms remain heterogeneous and poorly understood. Large-scale multiomics studies offer promise but are confounded by technical noise and extreme biological variance inherent to this condition. Pre-2026, standard analyses often treat outliers as artifacts, potentially discarding the most informative, high-risk biological signatures.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying a two-stage, anomaly-aware analytical framework to a preterm-enriched multiomics cohort will first robustly separate technical artifacts from true biological outliers, and second, that these curated biological outlier subgroups will harbor the most extreme and mechanistically informative molecular signatures of PTB risk and pathogenesis.
proposals.method: We will analyze an existing multiomics cohort (e.g., transcriptomics, proteomics, metabolomics) from preterm and term pregnancies. First, for quality control, we will use Isolation Forest and One-Class SVM on technical metrics to flag poor-quality samples. Second, on high-quality data, we will perform multiomics integration using MOFA+ to derive latent factors, then apply Local Outlier Factor (LOF) and autoencoder-based reconstruction error methods to identify biological outliers. These outlier subgroups will be characterized via differential expression and pathway analysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel computational pipeline for anomaly-aware multiomics analysis in clinical cohorts. It will produce a validated set of high-confidence, high-risk molecular signatures for PTB, moving beyond population-averaged signals. The framework will be a resource for studying other extreme-condition phenotypes where biological outliers are critical.
proposals.novelty: This approach innovatively reframes outlier detection from a purely technical QC step to an active biological discovery tool for PTB. It explicitly leverages extreme physiology as a signal, contrasting with pre-2026 methods that typically mitigate or discard outliers, thereby preserving potentially high-effect biological information.

proposals.title: In Vivo Validation of a Multiomic Machine Learning Model for Immune Modulation in a Murine Model of Alzheimer's Disease
proposals.motivation: Multiomic models, particularly those using deep learning on single-cell data, have identified promising immune cell signatures associated with resilience to complex diseases like Alzheimer's Disease (AD). However, these computational associations require causal validation in living systems to translate into viable therapeutic strategies. Testing predicted immune-modulating interventions in vivo is a critical, pre-clinical next step to bridge the gap between computational discovery and clinical application.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that interventions designed to shift the peripheral and central nervous system immune landscape towards the 'resilient' cell state profile predicted by our prior deep learning model will ameliorate cognitive and neuropathological deficits in a murine model of AD.
proposals.method: We will use a well-characterized transgenic mouse model of AD (e.g., 5xFAD). Based on our prior model's output, we will select 2-3 candidate interventions (e.g., specific cytokine modulators or repurposed immunomodulatory drugs) predicted to induce the resilient immune state. Treated and control cohorts will undergo longitudinal behavioral testing (e.g., Morris water maze). Terminal analysis will involve high-dimensional flow cytometry and CyTOF of blood, spleen, and brain immune cells, with data integration using the previously validated neural network architecture (a multi-input convolutional neural network for cell type profiles) to quantify the shift towards the target resilient state. Histopathological analysis will assess amyloid-beta plaque load.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first causal, in vivo evidence for immune-modulation strategies derived from a deep learning-based multiomic model in AD. It will move a computational signature from a correlative biomarker to a validated therapeutic pathway. The resulting dataset will also refine the original model by incorporating causal, longitudinal in vivo data.
proposals.novelty: While others have used machine learning to find associations, this proposal uniquely uses a pre-existing, disease-specific deep learning model to directly guide and validate targeted in vivo interventions, creating a closed-loop from computational discovery to biological validation.

proposals.title: A Multiomic Deep Learning Model of the First Trimester Immune-Metabolic Niche for Predicting Placental Health Trajectories
proposals.motivation: The first trimester is a critical window for establishing maternal-fetal tolerance and placental development, yet it remains a 'black box' due to limited longitudinal sampling. Pre-2026 studies, including Dr. Aghaeepour's work on predicting Placenta Accreta Spectrum (PAS), highlight that adverse pregnancy outcomes have roots in early gestation. A high-resolution model of this period is essential to identify the earliest deviations leading to complications like preeclampsia, preterm birth, and PAS, enabling proactive rather than reactive care.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a dense, longitudinal multiomic profile (transcriptomic, proteomic, metabolomic, and immune cell phenotyping) from maternal blood during the first trimester will reveal a dynamic, interconnected network of immune and metabolic signals whose specific trajectory is predictive of subsequent placental health and pregnancy outcomes.
proposals.method: We will perform weekly blood sampling from a prospective cohort from conception to 13 weeks. Samples will undergo CyTOF for deep immune phenotyping, bulk RNA-seq, Olink proteomics, and LC-MS metabolomics/lipidomics. Data integration and dynamic modeling will use a multi-task neural network architecture with attention mechanisms, building on methods from the Alzheimer's resilience paper. This model will learn to map temporal multiomic states to later clinical phenotypes, identifying critical inflection points and predictive early signatures.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first high-density, dynamic map of first trimester physiology, directly linking early molecular events to clinical outcomes. It will deliver a validated machine learning model for early risk stratification of placental disorders. Furthermore, it will identify novel, targetable biological pathways active during the establishment of pregnancy, shifting the paradigm for prenatal intervention.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has analyzed single time points or later gestation, the novelty lies in the intensive, weekly longitudinal design focused exclusively on the foundational first trimester, and the application of an integrated deep learning framework—previously used for neurological disease—to model the dynamic, multi-driver system of early pregnancy establishment.

proposals.title: Inverse Modeling of Immune Trajectories: Predicting Placental Pathology Risk from Deviations in Maternal Immune Cell Dynamics
proposals.motivation: Current models for predicting pregnancy complications like preeclampsia or placenta accreta spectrum (PAS) often rely on static biomarkers or late-stage clinical signs. Aghaeepour's prior work established that gestational immune trajectories are highly predictable in healthy pregnancies. This creates an opportunity to frame pathology prediction as an inverse problem: identifying risk based on deviations from the learned healthy trajectory, enabling earlier and more mechanistic risk assessment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a machine learning model trained to predict gestational age from maternal immune cell composition in healthy pregnancies will encode a robust 'healthy trajectory', and that the magnitude and nature of a new patient's deviation from this model will be predictive of specific placental pathology risks.
proposals.method: We will leverage existing high-dimensional flow/mass cytometry data from longitudinal maternal blood samples (healthy and pathological cohorts). First, a deep learning model (e.g., a recurrent neural network or transformer encoder) will be trained exclusively on healthy pregnancy data to regress immune features to precise gestational age. For new samples, we will compute a multi-dimensional 'deviation vector' capturing the error in the age prediction and the specific feature perturbations. This vector, combined with baseline clinical data, will serve as input to a second, pathology-specific classifier (e.g., XGBoost or a feed-forward network) to predict risk for preeclampsia and PAS.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel computational framework for pathology prediction based on deviation from a learned physiological norm, moving beyond associative models. It will provide specific, interpretable immune deviation signatures associated with distinct placental disorders, offering potential biomarkers for early intervention and a new paradigm for pregnancy health monitoring.
proposals.novelty: While Aghaeepour's group has used similar data to predict gestational age and identify disease associations, explicitly training a healthy model and using its failure mode (the inverse problem) as the direct input for pathology classification is a distinct, causality-suggestive approach not yet fully explored in perinatal immunology.

proposals.title: Temporal stratification of SSRI immunomodulation: A deep learning analysis of cellular responses across pandemic phases
proposals.motivation: Epidemiological studies have linked maternal SARS-CoV-2 infection and pandemic-related stress to altered birth outcomes, including preterm birth. Concurrently, selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) have shown promise as repurposed anti-inflammatory and antiviral agents. However, the cellular mechanisms of SSRIs have not been studied in the context of the evolving population-level immune landscape shaped by the pandemic, which is critical for understanding their potential therapeutic window and impact on perinatal health.
proposals.hypothesis: The anti-inflammatory and antiviral potency of SSRIs in primary human immune cells is modulated by the pandemic phase during which donor cells or serum are sourced, reflecting the population's shifting inflammatory and immunological milieu.
proposals.method: We will source peripheral blood mononuclear cells (PBMCs) and autologous serum from carefully curated biobanks representing three distinct phases: pre-pandemic (pre-2020), acute lockdown (2020), and later pandemic waves (2021-2023). PBMCs will be treated with common SSRIs (e.g., fluoxetine, sertraline) and challenged with viral mimics (e.g., poly(I:C)). High-dimensional single-cell cytometry data will be generated to profile immune cell states and cytokine production. We will employ a multi-task neural network architecture, building on our group's expertise, to model the interaction between SSRI treatment, pandemic phase (as a categorical embedding), and resulting cellular phenotypes, identifying phase-specific drug-response signatures.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first mechanistic, cellular-level evidence linking SSRI effects to the temporal dynamics of a global health event. It will identify specific immune pathways whose modulation by SSRIs is contingent on the population's immune context, offering a biological framework to interpret epidemiological trends in perinatal outcomes. The resulting deep learning model will serve as a prototype for analyzing drug effects across other temporally stratified cohorts.
proposals.novelty: While SSRI mechanisms have been studied, and pandemic-era immune changes documented, no study has experimentally fused these by stratifying cellular assays based on the precise pandemic phase, thereby directly testing a drug's action against a backdrop of population-level immunological history.

proposals.title: A Gestational-Age-Agnostic Deep Learning Model for Spontaneous Labor Prediction Using Maternal Immune and Metabolic Profiles
proposals.motivation: Accurate prediction of spontaneous labor onset remains a major challenge in obstetrics, with significant implications for preventing preterm birth and managing post-term pregnancies. Current models heavily rely on gestational age as a primary feature, which can be inaccurate and may obscure the underlying biological signals of labor readiness. This project aims to identify robust, age-independent biomarkers of labor, potentially enabling more precise clinical interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: Maternal peripheral immune cell composition and plasma metabolomic profiles contain sufficient biological signal to predict the onset of spontaneous labor within a defined window (e.g., 7-14 days) without using gestational age as an input feature.
proposals.method: We will leverage existing longitudinal cohorts with pre-labor maternal blood samples, mass cytometry (CyTOF) for deep immune phenotyping, and untargeted metabolomics. A multi-modal deep learning architecture, such as a late-fusion model with separate encoders for CyTOF cell frequency/profiles, metabolomic features, and baseline clinical variables (excluding gestational age), will be trained to predict labor onset. Model robustness will be tested using rigorous cross-validation and on held-out external cohorts.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, gestational-age-agnostic predictive model for labor, challenging a fundamental assumption in the field. It will identify specific immune cell states and metabolic pathways most predictive of labor, offering new mechanistic insights into the physiology of parturition and a foundation for clinically actionable tools.
proposals.novelty: This approach deliberately excludes gestational age, a canonical confounder, to force the discovery of intrinsic biological signatures of labor. It applies Aghaeepour's expertise in deep learning for cell-type-specific analysis to a new, critical problem in perinatal health, moving beyond disease classification to physiological event prediction.

proposals.title: Early-Warning Signals of Pregnancy Dysregulation: A Critical Transition Framework for Predicting Adverse Outcomes from First-Trimester Data
proposals.motivation: Adverse pregnancy outcomes like preterm birth and preeclampsia are major drivers of maternal and neonatal morbidity, yet reliable prediction often relies on late-pregnancy assessments, limiting preventive options. Current machine learning models for pregnancy risk, including our group's work on placenta accreta, typically require dense data from a narrow window near term. This proposal seeks to fundamentally shift prediction timelines by leveraging a powerful analogy from complex systems theory.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that pregnancy, as a complex physiological system, exhibits generic early-warning signals (increased variance, autocorrelation, critical slowing down) for adverse outcomes that emerge and stabilize during the first or second trimester, long before clinical symptoms manifest.
proposals.method: We will analyze existing longitudinal maternal multi-omics (e.g., metabolomics from IMiC-like cohorts) and wearable sensor data from conception. Using pre-2026 ecological methods, we will compute rolling-window statistics (variance, lag-1 autocorrelation, skewness) for key features over time. We will train a deep learning model (e.g., a recurrent neural network or transformer) to integrate these dynamic statistical signatures with baseline clinical data to predict outcomes. Model performance will be compared against traditional, late-pregnancy feature-based benchmarks.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, theory-driven framework for pregnancy risk stratification based on system dynamics rather than static biomarkers. It could enable earlier, sparser monitoring for foundational risk assessment, moving the field toward preventative care initiated in early pregnancy. It will also demonstrate the translational power of cross-disciplinary analogies from ecology to human health.
proposals.novelty: The application of critical transition theory and its statistical early-warning signals to human pregnancy is novel. It challenges the field's focus on late-pregnancy biomarkers by proposing that the predictive signal is not the biomarker level itself, but its dynamical properties much earlier in gestation.

proposals.title: Sparse, Early Cytometric Signatures from Rare Immune Phenotypes Predict Preterm Birth
proposals.motivation: Preterm birth (PTB) remains a leading cause of neonatal morbidity and mortality, with clinical prediction models lacking sensitivity and specificity. Current high-dimensional immune profiling in pregnancy focuses on dominant cell population averages, often requiring sampling near the event, which limits early intervention. A paradigm shift towards identifying fate-determining, rare cellular events could enable much earlier and more accurate risk stratification.
proposals.hypothesis: Rare, transient immune cell phenotypes present in maternal peripheral blood during early gestation contain sufficient predictive information for PTB, and these sparse signatures define a clinically actionable predictive window long before conventional biomarkers shift.
proposals.method: We will analyze existing high-dimensional flow and mass cytometry data from longitudinal pregnancy cohorts (e.g., IMiC consortium, Stanford cohorts) using a multi-step computational pipeline. First, we will employ deep learning-based cell type annotation (e.g., CellCNN, scVI) to resolve fine-grained phenotypes. Second, we will use rare-event detection algorithms and attention-based neural networks to identify sparse, outcome-associated phenotypic shifts across time. Finally, we will train gradient-boosted tree models (XGBoost) on these rare-feature trajectories to build a sparse early-prediction model for PTB.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, sparse biosignature for early PTB prediction derived from rare immune dynamics, moving the field beyond bulk population averages. It will provide a computational framework for rare-event-driven prediction in dynamically evolving clinical systems, with direct translational potential for early risk detection and intervention.
proposals.novelty: This challenges the prevailing assumption that robust prediction requires dense, high-frequency sampling of dominant cell states near a critical transition, instead positing that systematically identified rare phenotypes are sufficient for early prediction.

proposals.title: Iterative Refinement of a Minimal, Clinically-Viable Multiomic Biomarker Panel for Predicting Labor Onset Across Diverse Global Cohorts
proposals.motivation: Accurate prediction of labor onset remains a critical unmet need in obstetrics, with significant implications for managing preterm birth and scheduling elective deliveries. While multiomic models show promise, they are often complex and derived from homogeneous cohorts, limiting clinical translation. There is a pressing need for a simple, validated, and generalizable biomarker panel that can be deployed at the point of care across diverse populations.
proposals.hypothesis: A minimal biomarker panel, distilled from a high-performance multiomic model, can be iteratively refined using performance data from diverse, independent cohorts to achieve robust, generalizable prediction of labor onset while maintaining clinical utility.
proposals.method: We will apply the previously developed deep learning model (e.g., a multi-input neural network or gradient boosting machine) to large, independent cohorts from diverse demographic and geographic settings (e.g., IMiC Consortium partners). The initial minimal panel, derived via feature importance from the original model, will be validated. Performance disparities across cohorts will then drive an iterative refinement process using penalized regression (LASSO) and stability selection to identify universally robust core biomarkers versus context-specific auxiliary signals, optimizing the panel for each validation step.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated, evidence-based minimal biomarker panel ready for clinical assay development. It will provide a framework for translating complex multiomic models into practical tools and generate fundamental insights into the universality versus population-specificity of immunological and metabolic signals associated with parturition.
proposals.novelty: Moving beyond one-time validation, we propose an iterative, data-driven refinement loop where validation performance directly informs panel optimization. This ensures the final tool balances accuracy and generalizability by design, a crucial step for real-world implementation that is absent from most biomarker discovery studies.

proposals.title: Multi-Cohort Validation of an Integrated Multi-Omic and Clinical Model for Predicting Spontaneous Labor Onset
proposals.motivation: Accurate prediction of spontaneous labor onset remains a major challenge in obstetrics, with significant implications for managing term and preterm pregnancies. Existing models often lack generalizability due to being trained on homogeneous cohorts and limited biological features. Validating a predictive model in larger, diverse populations is a critical step toward a clinically useful tool for personalized pregnancy management.
proposals.hypothesis: An integrated model combining immune cell profiling (e.g., from maternal blood), metabolomic data, and clinical characteristics will maintain high accuracy for predicting time-to-labor onset when validated in independent, multi-ethnic, and geographically diverse cohorts.
proposals.method: We will apply a pre-trained model, likely a deep learning architecture (e.g., a multi-input neural network or gradient boosting machine) developed on prior single-cell RNA-seq, metabolomic, and clinical data, to at least two large, independent external cohorts. Model inputs will include standardized flow cytometry or deconvoluted bulk transcriptomic immune cell profiles, a core panel of serum metabolites/lipids, and structured clinical variables. Performance will be evaluated using time-to-event analysis (C-index) and calibration metrics, with stratification by race, ethnicity, and geographic site to assess fairness and robustness.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous assessment of the generalizability of a state-of-the-art labor prediction model, directly addressing a key translational gap. It will generate evidence for the model's clinical applicability across diverse populations and identify specific biological or social factors that influence prediction accuracy, informing future model refinements.
proposals.novelty: While multi-omic models for pregnancy outcomes exist, systematic external validation in large, intentionally diverse cohorts to test robustness and identify population-specific biases is not yet standard, moving beyond proof-of-concept in single datasets.

proposals.title: A Machine Learning-Driven Point-of-Care Immunoassay for Predicting Spontaneous Labor Onset Using a Maternal Blood Biomarker Panel
proposals.motivation: Accurate prediction of spontaneous labor onset remains a major challenge in obstetrics, with significant implications for managing preterm birth and scheduling elective deliveries. Current methods, like cervical exam or tocodynamometry, lack precision, leading to suboptimal clinical decisions and patient outcomes. A rapid, objective diagnostic test for imminent labor would transform prenatal care by enabling timely interventions and personalized management.
proposals.hypothesis: A previously identified multi-omic biomarker panel of immune and metabolic signatures, measurable in maternal peripheral blood, can be translated into a robust point-of-care lateral flow assay (LFA) whose quantitative output, when interpreted by a tailored machine learning model, will accurately predict the onset of spontaneous labor within a 7-day window.
proposals.method: We will first validate the candidate protein biomarkers (e.g., immune cytokines, pregnancy-specific proteins) using ELISA on a longitudinal cohort of patient serum samples. The most stable and predictive analytes will be adapted for multiplex detection on a lateral flow immunoassay platform. A compact reader will digitize the LFA signal intensities. We will then train a lightweight, interpretable machine learning model (e.g., Gradient Boosting Machine or a small neural network) on this quantitative multiplex data, integrated with basic clinical variables, to generate a labor probability score. The model will be optimized for edge deployment on the reader device.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a functional prototype of the first machine learning-integrated point-of-care test for labor prediction, moving beyond discovery to translational engineering. It will provide a validated framework for converting complex multi-omic signatures into simple, actionable clinical tools. The project will also generate a new public dataset of quantitative point-of-care biomarker readings linked to labor outcomes.
proposals.novelty: While prior work identified biomarker associations with labor, this proposal uniquely focuses on the engineering translation of a multi-analyte panel into a single, rapid diagnostic device augmented by embedded AI, a critical step not yet undertaken in perinatology. It merges Aghaeepour's expertise in immune phenotyping and machine learning with diagnostic device development.

proposals.title: Predictive Maintenance for Pregnancy: A Multi-Omics Time-Series Framework for Pre-Symptomatic Identification of Preterm Labor Trajectories
proposals.motivation: Preterm birth is a leading cause of neonatal mortality and long-term morbidity, yet current clinical prediction is largely reactive, occurring after symptomatic onset. This limits effective intervention. High-dimensional longitudinal biological data, analogous to multi-sensor streams in engineering, offers an untapped resource for modeling the dynamic trajectory of healthy pregnancy and its deviations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a normative, high-dimensional multi-omics trajectory of term pregnancy can be defined and that deviations from this trajectory, detectable via anomaly detection algorithms, will identify patients at risk for preterm labor significantly earlier than current clinical markers.
proposals.method: We will analyze dense longitudinal samples (maternal blood, cervicovaginal fluid) from existing cohorts (e.g., IMiC Consortium framework) with transcriptomic, proteomic, and metabolomic profiling. We will employ recurrent neural networks (RNNs) or Transformer-based models to learn the temporal dynamics of a 'normal operation' term pregnancy trajectory. Subsequently, we will apply unsupervised deep learning anomaly detection methods, such as variational autoencoders (VAEs) or one-class SVMs adapted for sequential data, to flag pre-symptomatic deviations predictive of preterm labor in held-out test sets.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first high-resolution, multi-omics 'reference trajectory' of late pregnancy. It will provide a novel computational framework for pre-symptomatic risk stratification, moving preterm birth prediction into a proactive phase. The identified early omics signatures will reveal new biological mechanisms of dysregulation.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct conceptual and methodological translation of predictive maintenance analytics from engineering to a complex biological system, applying state-of-the-art sequential anomaly detection to multi-omics pregnancy data to solve a critical, time-sensitive clinical prediction problem.

proposals.title: Longitudinal Multi-Omic Trajectory Mapping and Risk Stratification for Postpartum Recovery Complications
proposals.motivation: Postpartum recovery complications, including severe pain and persistent opioid use, represent a major yet understudied public health burden with significant long-term consequences for maternal health. Current clinical assessments are reactive and lack the granularity to predict individual recovery trajectories. Pre-2026 advances in high-throughput multi-omics and machine learning for conditions like preterm birth and chronic pain provide a framework to address this gap by moving from static snapshots to dynamic, predictive models of postpartum health.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that longitudinal multi-omic profiles (e.g., metabolomic, lipidomic, immune cell) integrated with clinical data will reveal distinct biological trajectories that can accurately predict an individual's risk for adverse postpartum recovery outcomes, including severe pain and opioid use persistence.
proposals.method: We will apply a trajectory analysis framework, inspired by preterm labor studies, to serial blood samples collected from patients post-delivery. Using deep learning architectures, such as recurrent neural networks (RNNs) or transformer-based models, we will model temporal omics patterns. These dynamic profiles will be fused with clinical variables using a composite risk score methodology adapted from chronic pain prediction studies to generate a longitudinal 'recovery risk trajectory' for each patient.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first predictive, biology-driven model of postpartum recovery, identifying high-risk trajectories for complications before they become clinically severe. It will provide a novel computational framework for integrating longitudinal multi-omics with clinical data for dynamic risk prediction in peripartum care, enabling targeted, pre-emptive interventions.
proposals.novelty: This proposal uniquely fuses the longitudinal multi-omics trajectory concept from obstetrics research with the composite risk prediction approach from chronic pain studies, applying it to the novel clinical domain of postpartum recovery—a critical but data-poor phase of maternal health.

proposals.title: Multi-omic trajectory analysis of the maternal-fetal interface reveals distinct immune and metabolic pathways preceding term and preterm labor
proposals.motivation: Preterm birth is a leading cause of neonatal mortality and long-term morbidity, yet its biological mechanisms remain poorly understood, limiting predictive and preventive strategies. Current clinical assessments are insufficient for early risk stratification. A comprehensive, longitudinal multi-omics analysis of the biological processes leading to labor can identify the specific pathways that diverge in preterm versus term parturition, offering critical insights into its etiology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that preterm labor is characterized by distinct, early deviations in the longitudinal trajectories of immune cell populations and metabolic profiles at the maternal-fetal interface, which can be identified through deep learning-based analysis of high-dimensional omics data.
