proposals.method: We will apply a multi-omics framework integrating single-cell RNA sequencing, plasma metabolomics, and lipidomics from serial maternal blood samples collected across gestation in cohorts with term and preterm labor. Using a neural ODE (Ordinary Differential Equation) network architecture, we will model the continuous biological trajectories for each patient. A contrastive deep learning model will be trained to identify the specific time-varying cell state and metabolic features that maximally differentiate the preterm from the term trajectory.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first high-resolution, dynamic map of immune and metabolic changes preceding term and preterm labor, identifying critical divergence points. It will deliver a validated, interpretable machine learning model for stratifying pregnancy trajectories based on early omics signatures, providing a foundation for future diagnostic tools and mechanistic studies.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies have compared static omics snapshots, this proposal uniquely applies neural ODEs to model the continuous trajectory of pregnancy, enabling the identification of the precise timing and nature of dysregulation leading to preterm birth, a dynamic process not captured by cross-sectional designs.

proposals.title: A Multi-Omic Graph Neural Network Framework for Predicting Fetal Health Outcomes from Paired Maternal and Placental Profiles
proposals.motivation: Pregnancy complications like preeclampsia and fetal growth restriction are major drivers of maternal and neonatal morbidity, yet their prediction and mechanistic understanding remain limited. Current approaches often analyze maternal blood alone, missing the critical fetal/placental component of the maternal-fetal dyad. Integrating matched maternal and placental omics data is essential for a complete systems immunology view of pregnancy health, but such paired datasets are rare and analytically challenging.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a computational model integrating paired maternal blood (e.g., transcriptomic, proteomic) and placental (e.g., transcriptomic, methylomic) multi-omics data will identify robust, biologically interpretable signatures that predict adverse fetal outcomes with greater accuracy than models using maternal data alone.
proposals.method: We will leverage existing cohorts with paired maternal blood and placental/villus samples. Data integration and modeling will use a graph neural network (GNN) architecture, where nodes represent molecular features (e.g., genes, proteins) and edges are defined by prior biological knowledge (e.g., protein-protein interactions, pathway membership). The model will have distinct but connected sub-graphs for maternal and placental data, allowing the network to learn cross-compartment interactions. We will employ attention mechanisms to identify the most predictive features and interactions for specific clinical outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, interpretable computational framework for analyzing paired maternal-fetal systems. It will produce a validated set of multi-compartment biomarkers for fetal health risk stratification. Furthermore, the model's attention weights will generate new biological hypotheses about maternal-fetal crosstalk mechanisms underlying pregnancy pathologies.
proposals.novelty: While multi-omic integration is common, applying a GNN to explicitly model the *interaction network* between two distinct biological compartments (maternal and placental) from paired samples is novel. This moves beyond simple concatenation of features to model the dyadic system's structure, aligning with the biological reality of pregnancy.

proposals.title: Parameterizing the Parturition Phase Transition: A Dynamical Systems Model of Labor Initiation Informed by Fetoplacental Multi-Omics
proposals.motivation: The precise mechanisms triggering human parturition remain elusive, limiting our ability to predict and prevent preterm birth. Current models predominantly analyze maternal signals in isolation, neglecting the fetoplacental unit as an active driver of the transition. A dynamical systems framework, which can mathematically model state transitions like the shift from pregnancy maintenance to labor, offers a powerful but under-explored approach for integrating multi-scale biological data into a causal model of this critical health event.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that multi-omics profiles from the fetal and placental compartments parameterize the potential function of a dynamical system describing pregnancy, and that specific shifts in these parameters drive a bifurcation corresponding to the destabilization of the pregnancy state and the initiation of labor.
proposals.method: We will analyze existing, matched maternal-fetal-placental multi-omics datasets (e.g., transcriptomics, methylomics) from longitudinal cohorts spanning late pregnancy. Using techniques from computational ecology, we will employ sparse regression (like SINDy) to infer ordinary differential equations governing key systemic mediators (e.g., inflammatory signals, hormone ratios). The fetal/placental omics features will be used to parameterize these equations via a supervised learning model (e.g., a regularized linear model or shallow neural network), explicitly linking compartment-specific biology to the system's stability landscape. We will then simulate the system to identify pre-transition signatures of critical slowing down.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first unified, mechanistic model of parturition that formally integrates fetal and placental drivers. It will yield interpretable, biologically-grounded parameters predicting the timing of labor onset, moving beyond associative maternal biomarkers. The framework will establish a new paradigm for studying other perinatal phase transitions, such as the onset of preeclampsia.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit use of fetal/placental omics to *parameterize* a dynamical model of a maternal physiological state transition, a direct causal formulation distinct from static correlation or maternal-only analyses. This fusion of developmental biology with ecological stability theory is unprecedented in perinatal research.

proposals.title: Causal Inference of the Gestational Clock: A Multi-Omic, Time-Aware, and Confounder-Robust Model for Ordering Preterm Labor Events
proposals.motivation: Preterm birth remains a leading cause of neonatal morbidity and mortality, yet its pathophysiology is poorly understood due to the complex, dynamic interplay of immune, endocrine, and tissue-remodeling pathways. Current studies, often based on single-timepoint or correlational multi-omic analyses, cannot distinguish the causal sequence of events that culminate in labor initiation, leaving critical predictive and therapeutic targets unidentified. A robust causal model of the pre-labor cascade is urgently needed to move beyond association and enable precise intervention.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating densely sampled longitudinal multi-omic data with time-aware causal discovery and genetic instrumental variable validation will reveal a conserved, temporally ordered cascade of molecular events driving the transition to labor, which is robust to unmeasured confounding.
proposals.method: We will apply time-lagged and constraint-based causal structure learning algorithms (e.g., PCMCI+, DYNOTEARS) to longitudinal transcriptomic, proteomic, and methylomic data from a well-phenotyped pregnancy cohort to construct preliminary dynamic Bayesian networks. To validate edges and infer direct causal effects, we will employ a two-sample Mendelian randomization framework, using pre-2026 published expression/protein quantitative trait loci (eQTLs/pQTLs) as genetic instruments to test putative causal relationships between molecular features and clinically relevant endpoints, such as inflammatory markers or cervical length.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first confounder-robust, dynamic causal map of the human pre-labor process, identifying key driver pathways and their temporal ordering. It will establish a novel analytical framework that integrates longitudinal multi-omics with genetic instrumental variables for causal discovery in perinatal health, directly extending Dr. Aghaeepour's prior work on predictive immune signatures into the causal domain.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synergistic combination of longitudinal, time-aware network inference with principled causal validation using Mendelian randomization, moving beyond the purely correlational or prediction-focused models that dominate pre-2026 multi-omic studies of pregnancy.

proposals.title: A Minimal, Clinically Actionable Biomarker Panel for Placenta Accreta Spectrum Derived from Multi-Omic Feature Selection
proposals.motivation: High-dimensional omics data, as used in our prior work on Placenta Accreta Spectrum (PAS), can identify robust predictive signatures but are impractical for rapid clinical deployment. A critical unmet need is to translate these complex research findings into a minimal, cost-effective, and interpretable biomarker set that can be validated in point-of-care settings, directly addressing the translational gap in maternal-fetal medicine.
proposals.hypothesis: A small, interpretable subset of metabolites, lipids, and clinical variables, identified through rigorous feature selection from a high-dimensional multi-omic profile, will retain equivalent predictive power for PAS as the full model, enabling clinical utility.
proposals.method: We will apply a multi-stage, embedded feature selection pipeline to our existing PAS metabolomic, lipidomic, and clinical dataset. Using SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) analysis on our previously developed gradient boosting models (e.g., XGBoost), we will rank feature importance. We will then iteratively refine the panel using LASSO regression and stability selection, validating the minimal set's performance against the full model via nested cross-validation. The final panel will be tested in a simplified logistic regression framework for clinical interpretability.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a directly testable, minimal biomarker panel for PAS, moving the field from discovery toward clinical assay development. It will establish a framework for distilling complex multi-omic deep learning signatures into practical tools, a methodology applicable to other pregnancy complications and immune-mediated conditions.
proposals.novelty: While feature selection is common, this proposal uniquely focuses on distilling a *clinical-grade* panel from a specific, validated PAS multi-omic predictor, prioritizing translational actionability over pure algorithmic performance, which is a non-trivial next step from our published work.

proposals.title: A Multi-Modal Deep Learning Framework for the Discovery of Subpopulation-Specific Immune-Metabolic Biomarkers of Labor Dysfunction from Nationwide EHR Data
proposals.motivation: Labor arrest is a leading cause of unplanned cesarean delivery, contributing significantly to maternal morbidity. Current clinical management lacks predictive tools and is based on population-averaged guidelines, which may not account for biological heterogeneity across diverse patient groups. Pre-2026, large-scale electronic health record (EHR) databases and multi-omics profiling offer an unprecedented opportunity to dissect this heterogeneity and move towards personalized obstetrics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that distinct, clinically definable patient subpopulations harbor unique predictive biomarker signatures—encompassing immune, metabolic, and hormonal features—for labor dysfunction, which are obscured in aggregate analyses.
proposals.method: We will train a multi-modal deep learning model on a nationwide EHR database (e.g., Cerner Real-World Data or PCORnet), integrating structured clinical variables (BMI, ethnicity, region) with high-dimensional biomarker panels (inflammatory cytokines, metabolomic/lipidomic profiles from stored samples). The core architecture will be a conditional prediction network, using subpopulation identifiers (e.g., via embedding layers) to modulate feature importance in a transformer or attention-based classifier. This will enable the model to learn subpopulation-specific predictive weights. We will employ SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) for interpretable, subgroup-specific biomarker discovery.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first national-scale, subpopulation-specific biomarker models for labor arrest, moving beyond one-size-fits-all prediction. It will generate actionable hypotheses about divergent etiologies of labor dysfunction (e.g., inflammatory-driven vs. metabolic-driven) in different groups. The validated biomarker panels could form the basis for future targeted diagnostic tests and personalized intrapartum intervention strategies.
proposals.novelty: While machine learning has been applied to obstetric prediction, the novelty lies in explicitly architecting the model to *discover* heterogeneous biomarker patterns as its primary output, using scale and diversity not as noise but as a discovery engine for population-specific pathophysiology.

proposals.title: Causal Disentanglement of Immune and Iatrogenic Signals in Placenta Accreta Spectrum Using Pathophysiology-Aware Machine Learning
proposals.motivation: High-risk pregnancy complications like Placenta Accreta Spectrum (PAS) fundamentally alter the maternal physiological and immune environment. However, the signals measured in patient cohorts are a complex mixture of the pathophysiology itself and the profound iatrogenic confounding from aggressive clinical management (e.g., early delivery, blood transfusions, corticosteroids). Disentangling these sources is critical for identifying true biological drivers of disease versus artifacts of treatment, which has been a major barrier to discovering predictive biomarkers and therapeutic targets.
proposals.hypothesis: By formally modeling the structured biases introduced by PAS pathophysiology and its standard clinical management as a causal graph, we can use targeted learning and adversarial debiasing to isolate a core set of immune and metabolomic features that are causally associated with the disease etiology, independent of treatment effects.
proposals.method: We will build a structural causal model (SCM) from expert clinical knowledge and literature to map confounding pathways. Using high-dimensional immune cell (e.g., mass cytometry) and metabolomic data from PAS and control cohorts, we will implement a Targeted Maximum Likelihood Estimation (TMLE) framework to estimate the average treatment effect of the disease. To further adjust for residual latent confounding from clinical interventions, we will integrate an adversarial neural network component into the feature extraction layer of a deep learning model, forcing it to learn representations predictive of PAS but invariant to treatment proxies. Validation will use synthetic counterfactual data generated from the SCM.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, pathophysiology-aware causal modeling pipeline for high-risk obstetrics, delivering a refined set of candidate biomarkers for PAS that are robust to clinical confounding. It will provide a methodological framework for other researchers to isolate disease-specific signals in similarly complex, heavily managed clinical conditions, moving beyond associative findings toward causal inference.
proposals.novelty: While causal inference and adversarial learning have been used separately, their integration within a formal SCM specifically designed for the unique, severe confounding structure of a surgical obstetric disease is novel. This moves past standard adjustment to actively model and strip out the iatrogenic signal, a challenge not addressed in prior PAS omics studies.

proposals.title: Re-evaluating the Critical Window: A Machine Learning Framework to Challenge the 16-Week Paradigm for Early Pregnancy Outcome Prediction
proposals.motivation: In prenatal and maternal-fetal health, early prediction of adverse outcomes like preeclampsia, preterm birth, or placenta accreta is crucial for intervention. The clinical and research focus has often centered on data from the first trimester (up to 16 weeks) as the presumed optimal window for early risk stratification. This assumption, however, may prematurely limit predictive power by excluding potentially informative, earlier immune and metabolic dynamics. Grounded in pre-2026 work on multi-omics and deep learning for clinical prediction, this proposal seeks to rigorously test this foundational assumption.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that predictive models built exclusively on data collected before 16 weeks of gestation are not optimal, and that models incorporating even earlier time points (e.g., pre-conception or <10 weeks) will demonstrate superior performance for specific adverse pregnancy outcomes.
proposals.method: We will leverage existing longitudinal cohorts with high-dimensional data (e.g., mass cytometry, metabolomics, clinical labs) collected at multiple pre-16-week time points. Using a multi-timepoint deep learning framework, such as a recurrent neural network (RNN) or transformer encoder, we will model the temporal evolution of immune and metabolic profiles. We will perform a rigorous comparative analysis, training and validating separate models on data truncated at progressively later windows (e.g., <8w, <12w, <16w) to predict predefined adverse outcomes. Performance will be evaluated using AUC-ROC and precision-recall curves.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a data-driven assessment of the 'critical window' for early pregnancy prediction, potentially shifting clinical sampling paradigms. It will deliver a validated, time-aware machine learning framework for analyzing very early pregnancy multi-omics data. The findings could identify specific immune or metabolic signatures in the earliest stages of gestation that are most predictive of later pathology.
proposals.novelty: While many studies use first-trimester data for prediction, few directly challenge the sufficiency of the 16-week window itself through comparative temporal modeling. This approach applies state-of-the-art temporal deep learning, common in other fields, to a fundamental yet untested assumption in perinatal immunology and predictive health.

proposals.title: A Longitudinal Immune Atlas of Pregnancy: Dynamic Profiling for the Prediction of Placental Dysfunction
proposals.motivation: Current models for predicting adverse pregnancy outcomes like Placenta Accreta Spectrum (PAS) and preeclampsia rely heavily on static, cross-sectional risk factors and late-stage clinical signs, limiting early intervention. The maternal immune system undergoes profound, orchestrated changes throughout gestation that are critical for healthy placentation, but these dynamic trajectories are poorly characterized in at-risk populations. Capturing this longitudinal immune state is essential for moving beyond static snapshots to identify early, predictive signatures of dysfunction.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that longitudinal, high-dimensional immune profiling during pregnancy will reveal dynamic cell state signatures that are more predictive of placental dysfunction than static clinical or metabolomic/lipidomic factors alone, enabling earlier and more accurate risk stratification.
proposals.method: We will perform deep immunophenotyping via mass cytometry (CyTOF) on serial peripheral blood mononuclear cell (PBMC) samples collected across trimesters from a well-characterized cohort, including patients with PAS, preeclampsia, and healthy controls. These longitudinal immune profiles will be integrated with clinical and metabolomic data. We will employ recurrent neural networks (RNNs), specifically Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks, to model the temporal evolution of immune cell populations and states. This model will be trained to predict clinical outcomes, with attention mechanisms used to identify critical time windows and cell subsets driving the predictions.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first high-dimensional, longitudinal map of immune dynamics associated with placental disorders, identifying novel temporal biomarkers. It will deliver a clinically actionable, time-aware machine learning model for early risk prediction. Furthermore, it will establish a framework for using dynamic immune trajectories as a fundamental component of pregnancy health assessment.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Aghaeepour and others has used single-timepoint omics for prediction, this study explicitly models the *temporal dynamics* of the immune system as a core predictive feature. The application of RNNs to model CyTOF-derived immune trajectories for pregnancy complications represents a significant methodological advance over static models.

proposals.title: A Prospective, Randomized Trial of a Deep Learning Model for Predicting Postpartum Hemorrhage Risk Using Immune and Metabolic Profiles
proposals.motivation: Postpartum hemorrhage (PPH) remains a leading cause of maternal morbidity and mortality worldwide. While machine learning models for predicting obstetric complications like placenta accreta have been developed, their real-world clinical utility and impact on patient outcomes are largely unproven. Prospective validation is the critical next step to translate predictive models from research tools into actionable clinical decision-support systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a prospective clinical trial, where clinicians are provided with a validated deep learning model's prediction of high PPH risk, will lead to earlier, targeted interventions and a significant reduction in severe PPH incidence and blood product utilization compared to standard care.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-center, randomized controlled trial. The intervention arm will use a previously developed deep learning model (e.g., a graph neural network or attention-based model) that integrates third-trimester peripheral immune cell profiling (mass cytometry) and plasma metabolomic/lipidomic data to generate a PPH risk score. Clinicians in this arm will receive the risk stratification for each patient. The primary outcome will be the rate of severe PPH (blood loss ≥1000mL). Secondary outcomes include units of blood transfused and admission to intensive care. Model performance will be continuously monitored for calibration and discrimination in the prospective cohort.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide Level I evidence for the clinical effectiveness of a multi-omics predictive model in obstetrics. It will establish a framework for the prospective deployment and evaluation of AI/ML tools in perinatal care. The findings will directly inform whether such models can be integrated into routine antenatal workflows to improve maternal health outcomes.
proposals.novelty: This moves beyond retrospective model development and validation to a prospective, outcome-focused clinical trial, which is rare for AI-based tools in perinatal medicine. It uniquely tests the hypothesis that providing a specific, biologically-grounded prediction directly alters clinical management and improves hard endpoints.

proposals.title: Causal Pathway Attribution in Preeclampsia: A Climate Attribution-Inspired Framework for Mapping Immune and Metabolic Drivers
proposals.motivation: Preeclampsia remains a leading cause of maternal and fetal morbidity and mortality, characterized by heterogeneous clinical presentations that suggest multiple underlying causal pathways. Current research, including our own work using deep learning on cell types and metabolomics, identifies strong associations but struggles to distinguish causal drivers from correlative signals, hindering the development of targeted interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying causal discovery algorithms from climate attribution science to high-dimensional, longitudinal maternal data will reveal distinct, probabilistically quantifiable causal pathways—specifically involving immune dysregulation, vascular maladaptation, and metabolic dysfunction—that lead to preeclampsia.
proposals.method: We will apply constraint-based causal discovery algorithms (e.g., PC and FCI algorithms) and score-based methods for Bayesian network learning to longitudinal datasets encompassing single-cell immune profiling, metabolomic/lipidomic data (similar to our placenta accreta work), and clinical characteristics from preeclampsia cases and controls. These methods will construct structural causal models to separate direct causal links from confounding noise, explicitly testing for the directionality and strength of relationships among immune cell states, metabolic markers, and clinical outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This work will move the field from associative biomarker discovery to a causal pathway attribution framework, identifying specific, intervenable chains of events in preeclampsia pathogenesis. It will provide a novel analytical paradigm for other complex pregnancy disorders and deliver a probabilistic model for patient stratification based on causal driver pathways.
proposals.novelty: The direct adaptation of causal discovery frameworks from climate extreme event attribution to a biomedical problem is novel, offering a rigorous method to disentangle causality in a system where randomized experiments are ethically impossible. This goes beyond standard machine learning correlation by explicitly modeling causal structure.

proposals.title: Temporal Causal Discovery from Prenatal Clinical Notes: Uncovering Interpretable Risk Pathways for Adverse Pregnancy Outcomes
proposals.motivation: Prenatal care generates rich, longitudinal clinical notes, but their unstructured nature obscures the temporal evolution of risk factors. Current machine learning models for pregnancy outcomes often act as black boxes, providing risk scores without revealing the dynamic, causal pathways that clinicians could intervene upon. An interpretable, causal framework is needed to move from correlation to actionable insight in maternal health.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying temporal causal discovery to structured concepts extracted from sequential prenatal notes will reveal interpretable, time-dependent risk pathways (e.g., specific symptom sequences leading to preeclampsia) that are more clinically actionable than static, black-box model features.
proposals.method: We will develop a hybrid NLP-causal system. First, a clinical concept recognizer (e.g., a fine-tuned BERT model or BioClinicalBERT) will extract symptoms, behaviors, and interventions with temporal anchors from visit notes. These will be mapped to a structured prenatal ontology (e.g., extending OMOP or PCORnet). We will then apply temporal causal discovery algorithms, such as PCMCI (Peter-Clark Momentary Conditional Independence) or a continuous-time structural equation model, to the resulting longitudinal patient trajectories to infer potential cause-effect relationships between variables over gestational time.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel methodology for deriving interpretable, temporal risk models from unstructured clinical text. It will generate specific, testable hypotheses about dynamic risk pathways in pregnancy, directly contributing to the field of causal inference in computational phenotyping. The output will be a set of clinician-evaluated risk pathways, advancing towards trustworthy AI in perinatal medicine.
proposals.novelty: While NLP for note extraction and causal discovery on structured data exist separately, their integration for dynamic pathway discovery in prenatal care is novel. This moves beyond using NLP for feature extraction for prediction, instead using it to build a temporal causal graph for explanation.

proposals.title: A Dynamic Accelerated Failure Time Model for Personalized Prediction of Preeclampsia Onset Using Longitudinal Immune and Metabolomic Profiling
proposals.motivation: Preeclampsia is a leading cause of maternal and fetal morbidity and mortality, yet current clinical screening (primarily first-trimester risk scores) is static and lacks precision in predicting the timing of disease onset. This limits opportunities for timely, personalized monitoring and intervention. The field lacks robust, dynamic models that treat pregnancy as a time-evolving physiological system and predict the specific gestational time of 'failure' (preeclampsia diagnosis).
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that modeling the maternal immune and metabolic adaptation to pregnancy as a system under stress, using an Accelerated Failure Time (AFT) framework, will enable accurate, personalized prediction of the time-to-preeclampsia onset from longitudinal biomarker data and clinical covariates.
proposals.method: We will adapt reliability engineering's AFT models, specifically a deep learning-based parametric survival network. The model will ingest time-series data from maternal blood samples (e.g., mass cytometry for immune cell states and metabolomic/lipidomic profiles) alongside static clinical covariates. A neural network will parameterize a log-normal or Weibull distribution for the time-to-event, where the scale parameter is a learned function of the longitudinal 'stressors' (biomarkers) and covariates. This will be trained and validated on existing longitudinal cohorts of pregnant patients, with preeclampsia diagnosis as the failure event.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, dynamic clinical risk tool that provides a personalized time-to-event risk profile for preeclampsia, moving beyond binary classification. It will identify specific longitudinal immune and metabolic signatures that accelerate or decelerate the time to clinical diagnosis, offering new insights into disease pathophysiology and potential windows for intervention.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the conceptual analogy from reliability engineering and the technical application of a deep AFT model to longitudinal biomedical data for time-to-event prediction in pregnancy, a significant advance over static risk scores or standard Cox models that do not inherently model the system's 'failure time' under observed dynamic stresses.

proposals.title: A Deep Learning Framework for Dynamic Risk Stratification of Hypertensive Disorders of Pregnancy Using Nationwide Electronic Health Records
proposals.motivation: Adverse pregnancy outcomes like preeclampsia remain leading causes of maternal morbidity, yet current risk assessment is largely static, based on single-timepoint measures. This fails to capture the dynamic, longitudinal evolution of risk throughout gestation, especially for rare but critical temporal patterns. Leveraging nationwide EHR data at scale is essential to model these complex trajectories and move towards proactive, personalized antenatal care.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that deep learning models applied to high-frequency, longitudinal EHR data can identify distinct, temporally evolving risk trajectories for hypertensive disorders of pregnancy, which will outperform static models and reveal novel, clinically actionable subphenotypes of disease progression.
proposals.method: We will utilize a large-scale national EHR database (e.g., a federated network like the NIH All of Us Research Program, pre-2026). We will employ a multi-modal deep learning architecture, specifically a Transformer-based model or a recurrent neural network with attention mechanisms, to integrate time-series data (vital signs, lab trends, medication sequences) with static baseline features. The model will be trained to predict the onset of hypertensive disorders, with interpretability techniques (e.g., SHAP, attention weights) used to identify critical time windows and feature interactions driving risk transitions.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated, dynamic risk stratification tool capable of providing personalized, trajectory-based alerts. It will contribute a publicly available computational framework for longitudinal pregnancy phenotyping and identify novel, data-driven subpopulations with distinct clinical courses, informing targeted intervention strategies.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying state-of-the-art temporal deep learning models to pregnancy EHR data at an unprecedented national scale, enabling the discovery of rare longitudinal patterns and moving beyond cross-sectional 'snapshots' to model risk as a continuous, evolving process.

proposals.title: A Continuous, Multi-Parameter Risk Index for the Spectrum of Hypertensive Disorders of Pregnancy Derived from Integrated Clinical, Biomarker, and Hemodynamic Data
proposals.motivation: Hypertensive disorders of pregnancy (HDP), including preeclampsia, are leading causes of maternal and fetal morbidity and mortality. Current risk assessment tools are often binary or categorical, failing to capture the continuous, multi-factorial nature of disease progression and the spectrum of severity. A more nuanced, probabilistic forecasting tool is urgently needed to enable personalized monitoring and timely intervention.
proposals.hypothesis: A composite risk index, integrating longitudinal multi-modal data, can provide a continuous, probabilistic forecast for an individual's position on the HDP severity spectrum, from gestational hypertension to severe preeclampsia/HELLP, with superior predictive accuracy compared to single biomarkers or clinical factors alone.
proposals.method: We will leverage a deeply phenotyped pregnancy cohort with serial measurements of clinical factors (BMI, medical history), angiogenic biomarkers (PlGF, sFlt-1), and hemodynamic data (uterine artery Doppler, BP variability). Inspired by seismology's composite forecasting, we will train a multi-task deep learning model (e.g., a recurrent neural network or transformer architecture) to jointly predict the probability of each HDP subtype across gestation. The model's latent representation will be calibrated into a single, interpretable HDP Risk Index (HDP-RI) scale.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, clinically actionable tool for continuous HDP risk stratification, moving beyond binary classification. It will provide a framework for integrating diverse data streams in perinatal health and identify key predictive trajectories across the HDP spectrum, informing mechanistic studies.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its direct analogy to geophysical forecasting to model a medical spectrum, and in its use of multi-task learning to generate a unified, continuous risk index for multiple related obstetric outcomes, rather than treating them as separate diagnostic endpoints.

proposals.title: A Multi-Task Deep Learning Framework for the Early Prediction of the Hypertensive Disorders of Pregnancy Spectrum Using Single-Cell Immune Profiling
proposals.motivation: Hypertensive disorders of pregnancy (HDP), including gestational hypertension, preeclampsia, and HELLP syndrome, are leading causes of maternal and fetal morbidity and mortality. Current risk stratification is imprecise, and these conditions are often diagnosed only after clinical symptoms manifest, missing a critical window for intervention. Pre-2026 work, including by Aghaeepour's group, has shown the predictive potential of immune and metabolomic data for specific conditions like placenta accreta, but a unified model for the entire HDP spectrum is lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a multi-task deep learning model trained on high-dimensional single-cell immune profiling data from early pregnancy can identify distinct and shared immune cell state signatures that accurately predict an individual's risk for developing specific hypertensive disorders across the HDP spectrum.
proposals.method: We will leverage existing and prospectively collected peripheral blood mononuclear cell (PBMC) CyTOF or single-cell RNA-seq datasets from pregnant patients. A multi-task neural network architecture with a shared encoder (e.g., a graph neural network or transformer for cell-cell interactions) and condition-specific prediction heads will be developed. This model will be trained to simultaneously predict the risks for gestational hypertension, preeclampsia with/without severe features, and HELLP syndrome as separate but related tasks, using pre-2026 frameworks like TensorFlow or PyTorch.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first computational tool for granular, early risk assessment across the entire HDP spectrum, moving beyond binary classification. It will identify condition-specific immune cell type profiles and shared pathways, offering mechanistic insights into disease etiology and progression. The model could be integrated into prenatal care to enable personalized monitoring strategies.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying a multi-task learning framework to single-cell immunology data to model the HDP spectrum as interrelated conditions, rather than treating them as separate binary outcomes. This approach captures the shared pathophysiology while discerning subtle immunological differences that dictate clinical phenotype.

proposals.title: A Causal, IL-1R4-Informed Machine Learning Score to Predict Postpartum Pain Chronification and Opioid Misuse Risk
proposals.motivation: Chronic postpartum pain and opioid misuse are major, interconnected public health crises. Pre-2026 research identifies inflammation as a key driver of pain persistence, but predictive models lack integration of specific, modifiable biological events occurring prior to pain onset. This gap limits the ability to deploy targeted preventative strategies during the critical peripartum window.
proposals.hypothesis: The magnitude and signaling profile of a pre-labor surge in the interleukin-1 receptor 4 (IL-1R4) pathway, when integrated with clinical and psychosocial data, causally contributes to postpartum pain trajectories and can form the basis of a clinically actionable risk score for chronic pain and opioid misuse.
proposals.method: We will leverage a longitudinal cohort of pregnant patients with serial blood samples from the third trimester through postpartum. Using high-dimensional flow cytometry and targeted proteomics (Olink), we will quantify pre-labor IL-1R4+ immune cell populations and associated inflammatory mediators. These immunologic features will be fused with electronic health record data and patient-reported outcomes. We will employ a causal forest algorithm to estimate heterogeneous treatment effects of the IL-1R4 'exposure' on pain outcomes, followed by a stacked ensemble model (e.g., XGBoost and penalized regression) to develop the final risk score, validated for discrimination and calibration.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first causal, inflammation-based risk algorithm for postpartum pain complications, directly identifying a pre-pain biological target for intervention. It will provide a framework for moving from associative to mechanistic prediction in peripartum health, with clear translational potential for anti-inflammatory prophylaxis.
proposals.novelty: Unlike existing associative models, this score is built around a specific, timed biological event hypothesized to be a causal driver, fusing causal inference with deep immunophenotyping in a clinical prediction framework. This shifts the predictive paradigm from static risk factors to modifiable biological processes.

ideas.title: Dual: Predict post-term birth and prolonged pregnancy
ideas.description: Apply the same framework to the opposite clinical problem: predicting pregnancies that will significantly exceed the expected delivery date.
ideas.sourceTitle: Crowdsourcing assessment of maternal blood multi-omics for predicting gestational age and preterm birth

ideas.title: Scale to predict a spectrum of hypertensive disorders
ideas.description: Expand the model's target to predict gestational hypertension and other related conditions.
ideas.sourceTitle: Early prediction of preeclampsia via machine learning.

ideas.title: Dual analysis of maternal and fetal immune compartments
ideas.description: Perform paired analysis of maternal blood and cord blood immune dynamics to study cross-talk.
ideas.sourceTitle: Differential Dynamics of the Maternal Immune System in Healthy Pregnancy and Preeclampsia

ideas.title: Scale down to model first trimester critical window
ideas.description: Apply high-density multiomic sampling specifically to the first trimester to model establishment of pregnancy.
ideas.sourceTitle: Multiomics modeling of the immunome, transcriptome, microbiome, proteome and metabolome adaptations during human pregnancy

ideas.title: Immune changes are primary cause, not secondary effect
ideas.description: Investigate if the observed immune dysfunctions are drivers of pathology or consequences of placental stress.
ideas.sourceTitle: Differential Dynamics of the Maternal Immune System in Healthy Pregnancy and Preeclampsia

ideas.title: Labor prediction independent of gestational age
ideas.description: Challenge the assumption that gestational age is a necessary confounder for all predictive models of labor.
ideas.sourceTitle: Integrated trajectories of the maternal metabolome, proteome, and immunome predict labor onset

ideas.title: Investigate mechanisms behind reduced preterm birth
ideas.description: Identify specific lockdown-related factors like reduced air pollution or stress that drove the small reductions in preterm birth.
ideas.sourceTitle: Changes in preterm birth and stillbirth during COVID-19 lockdowns in 26 countries

ideas.title: A Triangulation Framework Integrating Genetic Instrumentation with Longitudinal Multi-Omics to Establish Causal Pathways in Preterm Birth
ideas.description: This research system overcomes the fatal flaw of pleiotropy and weak instruments in traditional Mendelian randomization by deploying a causal triangulation strategy. It combines advanced genetic causal inference (multivariable MR, colocalization) with deep, serial molecular phenotyping from a prospective pregnancy cohort to create temporally-informed models. This integration allows findings from genetic instruments to be tested and reinforced by evidence from longitudinal mediation and natural experiment analyses within the same cohort. The result is a robust, multi-method pipeline that distinguishes causal drivers from correlative biomarkers by converging evidence across distinct methodological approaches with different underlying assumptions.

ideas.title: Integrate fetal or placental omics data
ideas.description: Combine maternal blood multi-omics with matched fetal or placental profiles for a complete systems view.
ideas.sourceTitle: Integrated trajectories of the maternal metabolome, proteome, and immunome predict labor onset

ideas.title: Develop a causal, inflammation-based risk score for postpartum pain chronification and opioid misuse
ideas.description: This research fuses the concepts of composite risk scoring and causal immunology to investigate the role of the pre-labor IL-1R4 surge as a key biological driver of postpartum pain trajectories. The goal is to develop a novel risk score that integrates the magnitude, triggers, and downstream signaling effects of this specific inflammatory event with clinical and psychosocial variables to predict which individuals are at highest risk for developing chronic postpartum pain or opioid misuse. This approach uniquely identifies a modifiable, pre-pain biological event as a core component of the risk algorithm, opening avenues for targeted anti-inflammatory interventions during the peripartum period to prevent pain chronification and its associated risks.

ideas.title: Causal Subtyping of Preeclampsia via Immune-Endophenotype Mendelian Randomization
ideas.description: This research direction applies causal inference methods, specifically Mendelian randomization, to test the hypothesis that preeclampsia comprises multiple distinct diseases with unique causal immune mechanisms. Instead of building separate predictive models based on clinical outcomes alone, it uses genetic instruments for specific immune features (e.g., cytokine levels, immune cell profiles) to probe for causal effects on preeclampsia risk within stratified patient populations defined by endophenotypes (e.g., placental pathology, angiogenic factor profiles). This approach will delineate whether observed clinical heterogeneity stems from different causal immune pathways, thereby defining biologically grounded subtypes. The result is a causal taxonomy of preeclampsia that can inform the development of targeted, pathophysiology-specific prevention and treatment strategies.

ideas.title: Dual analysis of maternal mental health
ideas.description: Scale the research focus from birth outcomes to the parallel changes in maternal mental health during the same period.
ideas.sourceTitle: Changes in preterm birth and stillbirth during COVID-19 lockdowns in 26 countries

ideas.title: Pathophysiology-Aware Causal Modeling for High-Risk Pregnancy Cohorts
ideas.description: This research system is designed to rigorously adapt methodologies for high-risk pregnancies by first formally modeling the confounding introduced by the complication's pathophysiology and clinical management. It employs causal inference techniques to adjust for these structured biases, isolating the specific signals related to the complication itself. The approach is validated using synthetic counterfactual data and stress-tested across varying clinical practice patterns. This ensures robust, clinically interpretable findings that are specifically relevant to the high-risk condition, not artifacts of the altered medical context.

ideas.title: E-cadherin/vimentin co-expression as a biomarker for placental maladaptation and stillbirth risk in low-resource settings
ideas.description: This research proposes that the co-expression of E-cadherin and vimentin in placental trophoblasts and decidual cells may not be a transient artifact but a maladaptive, stabilized hybrid state induced by environmental stressors prevalent in low and middle-income countries (LMICs), such as chronic infections, malnutrition, and inflammation. We will analyze placental tissue from stillbirth cases across diverse LMICs to test if this biomarker-defined state correlates with specific placental pathologies (e.g., malperfusion, chronic villitis) and stillbirth risk, independent of traditional causes. The synergy lies in using a nuanced molecular cell biology lens to uncover novel, mechanistically defined subtypes of stillbirth, moving beyond syndromic classification to identify targetable pathways for prevention in high-risk populations.

ideas.title: Compare trajectories in term vs preterm labor
ideas.description: Apply the same multi-omics approach to identify differences in biological trajectories preceding preterm labor.
ideas.sourceTitle: Integrated trajectories of the maternal metabolome, proteome, and immunome predict labor onset

ideas.title: Validate and Refine a Minimal, Clinically-Viable Biomarker Panel for Labor Onset Across Diverse Populations
ideas.description: This research direction simultaneously pursues validation and clinical translation by testing a distilled, minimal biomarker panel—derived from the original multiomic model—for predicting labor onset across large, independent, and demographically/geographically diverse cohorts. The key innovation is using the performance data from this broad validation not just for confirmation, but to iteratively refine the minimal panel itself, identifying which biomarkers are universally robust versus population-specific. This process ensures the final panel achieves optimal balance between predictive accuracy, clinical utility, and generalizability, moving beyond a one-time validation to create an adaptable, evidence-based tool ready for point-of-care implementation.

ideas.title: Elective Early Delivery as a Natural Experiment to Test the Clonality-Outcome Hypothesis in Placental Pathology
ideas.description: This study investigates whether the medically indicated shift in delivery timing during COVID-19 lockdowns—specifically, an increase in elective early-term deliveries—created a natural experiment to test the biological principle that reduced cellular phenotypic diversity correlates with adverse outcomes. We hypothesize that placentas from pregnancies electively delivered early, without the physiological signals of spontaneous labor, may exhibit lower cellular (e.g., trophoblast, stromal) phenotypic diversity and a more clonal architecture. By analyzing archived placental samples from pre-pandemic, lockdown, and post-lockdown periods, we will correlate quantified histopathological/molecular diversity metrics with neonatal and maternal outcomes to determine if elective timing itself induces a 'less fit,' clonal placental phenotype, thereby directly linking a population-level healthcare decision to tissue-level ecology and clinical results.

ideas.title: Dual-System Monitoring of Maternal Health: A Biophysical Parallel
ideas.description: This idea scales the research focus from birth outcomes to include parallel changes in maternal mental health by drawing an analogy from structural engineering. In that field, a dual-sensor system monitors a primary load-bearing structure and a parallel, degrading material system simultaneously to predict failure. Adapted here, the research would implement a dual-analysis framework treating maternal physical health and birth outcomes as the primary system and maternal mental health as the interdependent parallel system. By using synchronized, longitudinal data streams—such as physiological stress biomarkers, birth complication data, psychological assessments, and neuroendocrine profiles—the study aims to model how the 'load' of pregnancy and childbirth predicts distinct, often latent, trajectories of mental health change, providing a holistic, predictive view of maternal well-being akin to structural health monitoring.

ideas.title: Adapting Reliability Engineering's Failure Time Analysis for Preeclampsia Onset Prediction
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from Reliability Engineering, where survival analysis models predict the time until a mechanical system fails under specific stressors. The analogous problem is predicting component failure time using covariates like material stress and operating conditions. The adapted research idea treats the maternal physiological adaptation to pregnancy as a system and preeclampsia as its failure. By applying an Accelerated Failure Time model framework from reliability studies, we can use longitudinal maternal biomarker data and clinical covariates as input 'stressors' to generate personalized, dynamic predictions for not just if, but when during gestation preeclampsia is likely to occur, providing a clinically actionable time-to-event risk profile.

ideas.title: Predictive multi-omics trajectories for postpartum pain and recovery complications
ideas.description: This research applies the multi-omics trajectory analysis used to study preterm labor to map the biological pathways of postpartum recovery following both term and preterm deliveries. Instead of a single outcome, it uses the composite risk score methodology from chronic pain prediction to integrate these dynamic omics profiles with clinical data, creating a longitudinal 'recovery risk trajectory'. This allows for the early identification of individuals on a path toward severe postpartum pain, persistent opioid use, or other recovery complications, facilitating timely, targeted interventions to alter that trajectory.

ideas.title: Longitudinal Pregnancy Trajectory Modeling on a National EHR Scale
ideas.description: This research direction leverages a nationwide electronic health record database to train models that identify and characterize longitudinal risk trajectories throughout pregnancy. By integrating high-frequency, dynamic data (e.g., vital signs, medication changes, lab results, healthcare utilization patterns) with static baseline factors, the model captures how risk evolves over time for different subpopulations. This scale allows for the identification of rare but critical temporal patterns and interaction effects that are invisible in smaller cohorts or cross-sectional analyses. The outcome is a dynamic risk stratification system that can provide timely, personalized alerts and interventions based on a patient's specific trajectory rather than static risk profiles alone.

ideas.title: A Multi-Spectrum Predictive Scale for Hypertensive Disorders of Pregnancy Using a Composite Risk Index
ideas.description: This research expands the original seed idea by drawing an analogy from seismology's method of predicting a spectrum of seismic events. Just as geologists combine diverse precursor data into a composite forecast index for earthquakes and related tremors, we propose to develop a Hypertensive Disorder of Pregnancy (HDP) Risk Index. This index would be a continuous, multi-parameter scale that integrates maternal factors (e.g., BMI, medical history), serial biomarker measurements (e.g., PlGF, sFlt-1, PAPP-A), and hemodynamic data (e.g., uterine artery Doppler, blood pressure variability). The output would not be a single diagnosis but a position on a risk spectrum, providing a probabilistic forecast for the entire range of hypertensive disorders in pregnancy, from mild gestational hypertension to severe preeclampsia and HELLP syndrome, enabling more nuanced risk stratification and targeted monitoring.

ideas.title: Critical Transition Forecasting: Adapting Early-Warning Signals from Ecology to Pregnancy
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that a 100-day window near term is necessary or sufficient for prediction by drawing an analogy from ecosystem stability research. In ecology, systems approaching a critical transition (like a collapse) exhibit generic early-warning signals (increased variance, autocorrelation, slowed recovery) detectable during the preceding stable state, not just immediately before the shift. We adapt this solution by hypothesizing that pregnancy, as a complex physiological system leading to the 'transition' of birth and postpartum outcomes, may exhibit similar statistical precursors. The adapted research would analyze longitudinal maternal data (e.g., from wearables, routine tests) from conception onward to identify if such early-warning signatures for adverse outcomes (like preterm birth or preeclampsia) emerge and stabilize during early or mid-pregnancy, potentially making the 100-day window redundant for initial risk stratification and shifting focus to earlier, sparser sampling for foundational prediction.

ideas.title: National-Scale Discovery of Subpopulation-Specific Biomarkers for Labor Dysfunction
ideas.description: This research direction trains the inverse prediction model for labor delay/arrest on a nationwide EHR database, leveraging its immense scale and diversity. The key synergy is the ability to systematically identify and validate heterogeneous biomarker patterns—such as specific inflammatory, metabolic, or hormonal signatures—that are predictive of dysfunctional labor only within particular patient subgroups (e.g., defined by ethnicity, BMI class, or regional health factors). This approach transforms a general prediction task into a mechanism for discovering population-specific etiologies of labor arrest, which could inform targeted interventions and personalized clinical guidelines.

ideas.title: Scale down to a minimal predictive biomarker set
ideas.description: Identify the smallest subset of metabolites and proteins that retains robust predictive power for clinical utility.
ideas.sourceTitle: Integrated trajectories of the maternal metabolome, proteome, and immunome predict labor onset

ideas.title: Anomaly-Aware Multiomics for Preterm Biology: Using Outlier Detection to Distinguish High-Risk Signatures from Technical Noise
ideas.description: This research direction applies advanced anomaly and outlier detection algorithms to a large, preterm-enriched multiomics cohort with a dual purpose. First, it performs robust quality control to flag and remove poor-quality samples. Second, and more innovatively, it treats the remaining high-quality data to identify subgroups of samples that are statistical outliers based on their integrated molecular profiles. The core hypothesis is that within these biologically anomalous subgroups lie the most extreme and informative signatures of preterm birth risk and pathogenesis. This approach moves beyond simply cleaning data to actively mining it for high-risk biological signal, increasing the power to discover predictive biomarkers in a population inherently characterized by extreme physiological states.

ideas.title: Mechanistic cellular study of SSRI effects stratified by pandemic phase
ideas.description: This study investigates the anti-inflammatory and antiviral effects of SSRIs in primary human cells, but crucially stratifies the cellular experiments based on the pandemic phase from which donor cells or serum are sourced. By sourcing biological samples from pre-pandemic, acute lockdown, and subsequent pandemic waves (including periods of variants, vaccines, and changing treatments), the research can test if the cellular potency of SSRIs varies with the evolving immune and inflammatory milieu of the population. This approach directly connects a detailed molecular mechanism to the full temporal scale of the pandemic, offering a biological hypothesis for observed changes in birth outcomes like preterm birth rates over time.

ideas.title: Scale up to population-level screening programs
ideas.description: Design and cost a large-scale screening program using the immune signature in routine prenatal care.
ideas.sourceTitle: Differential Dynamics of the Maternal Immune System in Healthy Pregnancy and Preeclampsia

ideas.title: Validate predictive model in larger, diverse cohorts
ideas.description: Test the integrated model's accuracy for predicting labor onset in independent, larger, and more diverse populations.
ideas.sourceTitle: Integrated trajectories of the maternal metabolome, proteome, and immunome predict labor onset

ideas.title: Applying Predictive Maintenance Analytics from Engineering to Preterm Labor Trajectories
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from predictive maintenance in industrial engineering to the problem of preterm labor. In engineering, multi-sensor data streams are used to model normal equipment operation and detect early deviations predictive of failure. The adapted research idea applies this analogical framework to pregnancy. It involves collecting dense longitudinal multi-omics data (e.g., transcriptomics, proteomics, metabolomics, microbiome) from maternal blood, urine, or cervicovaginal fluid across gestation to first define the precise, high-dimensional biological trajectory leading to term labor. Advanced time-series and anomaly detection algorithms, similar to those used for machinery, would then be applied to this 'omics' sensor data to identify the earliest and most reliable deviations from the normative trajectory that signal an impending preterm labor event, moving prediction from symptomatic stages to a truly proactive, pre-symptomatic phase.

ideas.title: Prospective clinical trial of the prediction model
ideas.description: Conduct a prospective study to evaluate the model's clinical utility and impact on outcomes.
ideas.sourceTitle: Early prediction of preeclampsia via machine learning.

ideas.title: Fetal-Maternal Dynamical Systems: Using Fetal/Placental Omics to Parameterize Phase Transitions in Labor Initiation
ideas.description: This research direction integrates matched maternal, fetal, and placental multi-omics profiles into a dynamical systems framework adapted from ecology. By treating the fetoplacental unit as a critical regulatory compartment, its omics data (e.g., transcriptomics, methylomics) are used to parameterize the forces and potential functions in models of phase transition, such as bifurcations or critical slowing down. This creates a unified, causal model where shifting fetal/placental signals are formally linked to the destabilization of the maternal pregnancy maintenance state, offering a mechanistic prediction of the transition to labor not possible by modeling maternal signals alone or by analyzing omics data statically.

ideas.title: Leverage Extreme Event Analysis from Climate Science to Model Preterm Birth Signatures
ideas.description: This idea adapts the approach climate scientists use to predict rare, high-impact weather events to the problem of predicting preterm birth. In climate science, models for events like hurricanes use extreme value theory and enrich datasets with historical extreme events to identify precursor atmospheric patterns. Adapted here, we apply the multiomics framework to a large, preterm-enriched cohort, but with a specific analytical shift: treat preterm birth as a developmental 'extreme event.' We would employ extreme value statistical methods to model the tails of the gestational age distribution, actively searching for predictive multiomic 'precursor patterns' that signal a high probability of early parturition, moving beyond standard comparative analysis to a specialized, risk-tailored predictive model.

ideas.title: Network Control Theory for PTB: From Predictive Topology to Targeted Intervention
ideas.description: This idea applies the solution from Electrical Engineering's Network Control Theory, where predictive models of grid topology are used to identify and then physically harden critical nodes to prevent cascading failures. The adapted research idea for Preterm Birth (PTB) prevention involves constructing a high-dimensional predictive model (e.g., multi-omics network) of the biological system of pregnancy. Instead of using it solely for risk prediction, the model is employed to solve an inverse problem: running in silico perturbation analyses to pinpoint the most influential 'control nodes' whose dysregulation could trigger the PTB cascade. The core experimental shift is then to directly and mechanistically perturb these identified key nodes—such as specific immune signaling axes or microbiome-derived mediators—in preclinical model systems (e.g., murine models of inflammation-driven PTB). The goal is to test whether targeted stabilization of these high-leverage points, informed by the network model, can prevent the transition to the pathological state, thereby moving from correlative prediction to causal, mechanism-based intervention.

ideas.title: Static risk factors are most informative
ideas.description: Relax the focus on static variables by incorporating dynamic, longitudinal data from pregnancy.
ideas.sourceTitle: Early prediction of preeclampsia via machine learning.

ideas.title: Adapt Causal Discovery from Climate Attribution to Preeclampsia Pathways
ideas.description: This idea draws an analogy from the field of climate science, specifically extreme event attribution, which uses causal discovery and structural causal models to identify pathways linking human activity to specific climate events. The analogous solution involves separating signal from noise in complex systems. Adapted to preeclampsia research, this involves applying similar causal discovery algorithms (e.g., PC, FCI, or score-based methods for Bayesian networks) to longitudinal maternal health data. The goal is to map and probabilistically attribute the onset of preeclampsia to distinct, quantifiable causal pathways—such as those originating from immune dysregulation, vascular maladaptation, or metabolic dysfunction—thereby moving beyond correlation to identify potentially intervenable causal chains.

ideas.title: Causal NLP for Prenatal Notes: Extracting Interpretable Temporal Risk Pathways
ideas.description: This research develops a hybrid system that first applies NLP to extract clinical concepts and their temporal evolution from sequential prenatal visit notes. These observations are then mapped to a structured prenatal care ontology and modeled using temporal causal discovery algorithms to infer potential cause-effect relationships between symptoms, behaviors, interventions, and outcomes. The final output is a set of interpretable, time-dependent risk pathways (e.g., 'emerging hypertension noted at visit 20-24 weeks precedes proteinuria annotation at visit 28-32 weeks') rather than black-box features. This approach prioritizes clinical plausibility and actionable insight, enabling risk models that clinicians can understand, trust, and act upon.

ideas.title: Longitudinal Multi-Omic Causal Discovery with Confounder-Robust Validation for Pre-Labor Event Ordering
ideas.description: This adversarially improved research system is designed to infer the causal cascade of pre-labor events by analyzing densely sampled longitudinal multi-omic data (e.g., transcriptomics, proteomics, methylomics) from the same cohort of pregnant individuals across gestation. It employs time-aware causal structure learning to build preliminary dynamic networks that capture temporal dependencies. To overcome the fatal flaw of hidden confounding, the system then rigorously validates these inferred causal relationships using genetic instrumental variables (e.g., eQTLs, pQTLs) in a mendelian randomization framework, which provides a natural experiment to test for direct causal effects. The final output is a robust, confounder-adjusted causal model of the pre-labor process, significantly more reliable than correlations from static data.

ideas.title: The Rare-Cell Window: Early, Sparse Phenotypic Shifts as Sufficient Predictors of Systemic Outcome
ideas.description: This research challenges the paradigms that predictive signals require high-frequency sampling near a critical transition and that dominant cellular subsets dictate outcome. It posits that in dynamically evolving systems like pregnancy and cancer, rare, transient cell phenotypes—analogous to early metastatic seeds or unique decidual immune states—emerge decisively early and contain sufficient information for robust prediction. The core hypothesis is that identifying these critical phenotypic shifts, rather than monitoring bulk population averages, allows the definition of a sufficient 'predictive window' much earlier in the process. This enables the development of sparse, early biosignatures for outcomes like preterm birth or metastatic relapse, shifting clinical focus from late-stage, dense monitoring to early, targeted detection of fate-determining rare events.

ideas.title: Single-cell immune trajectory mapping for dynamic prediction and precision targeting of preventive therapy
ideas.description: This research direction integrates single-cell RNA sequencing of longitudinal maternal blood samples with clinical response data to preventive therapy. It moves beyond static signature-based stratification to model how specific immune cell subtype trajectories (e.g., monocyte priming, Treg dysfunction) are altered by the intervention in responders versus non-responders. This reveals the precise cellular mechanisms of successful prevention, distinguishing causal drivers from correlative bystanders. The resulting model dynamically predicts response likelihood based on pre-treatment cellular states and identifies novel, cell-specific molecular targets for adjunctive or alternative therapies in predicted non-responders.

ideas.title: Solve inverse problem: predict pathology from deviation
ideas.description: Instead of predicting gestational age, predict pathology risk from deviations from the healthy model.
ideas.sourceTitle: Multiomics modeling of the immunome, transcriptome, microbiome, proteome and metabolome adaptations during human pregnancy

ideas.title: A hybrid trial-design platform to evaluate interventions for pregnancy loss and maternal health in low-resource settings
ideas.description: This research proposes to design and implement a large-scale, pragmatic, platform randomized controlled trial (RCT) in several low and middle-income countries (LMICs) to evaluate a bundle of low-cost, feasible interventions aimed at reducing stillbirths. Unlike a traditional single-drug RCT, this platform would use an adaptive design to test multiple components (e.g., community-based screening, aspirin for pre-eclampsia prevention, specific infection management protocols) both individually and in combination within the same overarching trial infrastructure. The synergy lies in applying the gold-standard causal inference methodology of large clinical trials to the systemic challenge of stillbirths, generating high-quality evidence on what actually works in LMIC health systems. This moves beyond observational analysis and isolated trials to efficiently identify effective, scalable intervention packages tailored to resource-constrained environments.

ideas.title: Data before 16 weeks is sufficient for prediction
ideas.description: Challenge the assumption that data only from the first 16 weeks is optimal for early prediction.
ideas.sourceTitle: Early prediction of preeclampsia via machine learning.

ideas.title: Decompose the Predictive Signal: A Single-Cell Atlas of Maternal Immune Dynamics
ideas.description: This idea applies an analogy from astrophysics, where the light from celestial objects is decomposed via spectroscopy to identify specific contributing elements and sources. The analogous solution is the use of large-scale spectroscopic surveys to resolve a composite signal into its fundamental parts. Adapted to the original research context, we propose using single-cell RNA sequencing on maternal blood as a biological 'spectrometer' to deconvolve the predictive immune signature. This high-resolution approach will pinpoint the exact immune cell subtypes (e.g., specific monocyte, T-cell, or NK-cell subsets), their activation states, differentiation trajectories, and interaction networks that are the true drivers of the signature, moving from a bulk, averaged measurement to a precise, causal cellular atlas.

ideas.title: Develop point-of-care test from biomarker panel
ideas.description: Translate the identified biomarker panel into a rapid, clinically usable diagnostic test for labor prediction.
ideas.sourceTitle: Integrated trajectories of the maternal metabolome, proteome, and immunome predict labor onset

ideas.title: Investigate mechanistic role of STAT5ab signaling
ideas.description: Determine the functional role of abnormal STAT5ab signaling in CD4+ T cells in preeclampsia pathogenesis.
ideas.sourceTitle: Differential Dynamics of the Maternal Immune System in Healthy Pregnancy and Preeclampsia

ideas.title: Neighborhood-level multi-omic biosensors for environmental health equity
ideas.description: This research fuses hyper-local spatial epidemiology with minimal, clinically viable biomarker discovery to develop and validate a point-of-care diagnostic panel specifically for neighborhood-level environmental health assessment. The goal is to identify a minimal set of proteins or transcripts from maternal or cord blood that robustly reflect the synergistic impact of localized environmental stressors (e.g., air pollution, noise, green space access) and social determinants on adverse birth outcomes. By grounding biomarker discovery in granular neighborhood-level data during a defined stressor period like lockdowns, the resulting panel would provide a cost-effective tool for community health screening, enabling the identification of high-risk micro-environments and the evaluation of targeted place-based interventions.

ideas.title: Anomaly-Aware Biological Pathway Analysis for Robust Prenatal Biomarker Discovery
ideas.description: This research direction integrates industrial sensor anomaly detection algorithms with functional biological pathway analysis. The core innovation is to treat longitudinal molecular profiling data (e.g., transcriptomics, proteomics) from prenatal studies as a multi-stream sensor network, where each biological pathway's activity is a 'sensor signal'. Advanced transfer anomaly detection models are trained to identify deviations caused by instrument drift or reagent lot changes. By first filtering out these technical 'anomalies', the subsequent analysis of leukocyte-mediated immunity and other pathways related to preterm prelabor rupture of membranes (PPROM) becomes more precise and robust, leading to the identification of predictive signatures that are biologically driven rather than technically confounded.

ideas.title: In vivo analysis of immune-modulating interventions
ideas.description: Test interventions targeting the immune-modulating mechanisms identified by the multiomics model in living systems.
ideas.sourceTitle: Multiomics modeling of the immunome, transcriptome, microbiome, proteome and metabolome adaptations during human pregnancy

ideas.title: Challenge sufficiency of peripheral blood measurements
ideas.description: Test if local tissue-specific omics from placenta or uterus are required for a complete model.
ideas.sourceTitle: Multiomics modeling of the immunome, transcriptome, microbiome, proteome and metabolome adaptations during human pregnancy

ideas.title: Identify Endogenous Resilience and Simulate Personalized Preventive Interventions
ideas.description: This research integrates two inverse modeling approaches on the same high-risk cohort data. First, it identifies factors associated with not developing preeclampsia (endogenous resilience). Second, it models factors predicting a strong response to aspirin prophylaxis. By comparing these profiles, the study can classify high-risk patients into those protected by inherent biology, those protected by aspirin, those requiring alternative interventions, and those remaining vulnerable. This synergistic framework moves beyond prediction to a mechanistic understanding of protection, enabling the design of tailored, evidence-based prevention pathways that match intervention to the underlying protective deficit.

ideas.title: Resilience Profiling in High-Risk Pregnancies via Ecological Succession Models
ideas.description: This idea applies the inverse problem-solving framework from ecology's study of ecosystem resilience to the context of preeclampsia. In ecology, a key method is to identify why some high-risk, degraded ecosystems recover and become stable by profiling the protective factors present in resilient systems. Adapted here, the research would use existing high-risk pregnancy cohort data to model and identify the biological, clinical, and behavioral factors associated with not developing preeclampsia. The goal is to define a 'resilience profile' by focusing on the success stories, thereby uncovering novel protective mechanisms that could inform preventative strategies, mirroring how ecologists use successional models to inform restoration practices.

ideas.title: Adapting Planetary System Modeling to Feto-Placental-Maternal Integration
ideas.description: The original idea proposes combining maternal blood multi-omics with matched fetal or placental profiles for a complete systems view of pregnancy. Drawing an analogy from astrophysics, where models integrate direct stellar data with indirect planetary signals to understand entire systems, we adapt this solution. The adapted research idea treats the maternal circulation as the directly observed central component and the feto-placental unit as a partially observable subsystem. By using matched, direct fetal/placental omics profiles (e.g., from cord blood, placental biopsies) as ground-truth calibration points, we can develop a computational integration framework. This framework will model the relationships and signal contributions between compartments, effectively deconvolving the maternal blood omics to infer fetal and placental health states. The goal is to create a validated, predictive 'pregnancy system model' that enables future non-invasive, comprehensive monitoring using only maternal blood samples.

ideas.title: Maternal blood is the optimal predictive biospecimen
ideas.description: Test if other maternal biospecimens (e.g., cervical fluid, urine) or fetal/placental signals provide superior or complementary predictive signals for PTB.
ideas.sourceTitle: Crowdsourcing assessment of maternal blood multi-omics for predicting gestational age and preterm birth

ideas.title: Scale down: Prenatal molecular trajectories as biomarkers for congenital pain insensitivity
ideas.description: This research fuses the concepts by applying high-density multiomic sampling of the first-trimester placental-fetal interface to model the establishment of developmental trajectories specifically in pregnancies where the fetus carries a monogenic mutation for a rare congenital pain insensitivity disorder (e.g., NTRK1, SCN9A). By focusing on this genetically homogeneous, ultra-rare condition during the precise window of initial nervous system and placental programming, the study aims to identify the earliest molecular biomarkers of the disorder. This approach moves beyond postnatal symptom correlation to uncover the foundational developmental pathways that are derailed, offering a template for understanding how other rare neurodevelopmental disorders may originate in utero. The synergy lies in using the 'clear genetic signal' of the pain disorder to decode the 'noisy' critical window of early pregnancy, revealing primary etiological mechanisms rather than secondary consequences.

ideas.title: A Dynamical Systems Framework for Labor Prediction: Identifying Critical Transition States from Multi-omic Temporal Trajectories
ideas.description: This project abandons the search for static pre-labor subtypes in favor of modeling the dynamic, interacting systems that culminate in parturition. We will collect dense longitudinal multi-omic and physiological data from a large, diverse cohort throughout the third trimester to capture the precise temporal evolution toward labor. Using computational techniques from dynamical systems theory and causal inference, we will identify critical transition states—points of systemic instability—and the convergent network motifs that precede them. The goal is to build a predictive model of labor timing and character based on the real-time trajectory of the system, not on a fixed molecular classification.

ideas.title: Spatially map fetal immune signals driving maternal systemic immune remodeling in pregnancy-associated cancers
ideas.description: This fused idea applies spatial transcriptomics to investigate the 'inverted' immune-fetal interaction within the context of pregnancy-associated cancers, such as breast cancer. It aims to locate rare fetal cell subsets (e.g., fetal macrophages, trophoblasts) or their signaling niches within the maternal tumor architecture and adjacent tissue. By correlating these precise spatial locations with transcriptomic signatures of local and systemic maternal immune cell states, the research can directly test the hypothesis that fetal signals act as localized organizers of immune privilege or dysfunction.
